vulnerability_text: The facility does not use barriers to mitigate high-speed avenues of approach. Barriers would reduce vehicle speeds and/or prevent vehicle penetration.

options.option_text: Evaluate vehicle traffic patterns near the facility. Design and implement strategies to reduce vehicle speeds, improve pedestrian safety, and reduce the threat of vehicle approach velocities.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security, Guidance Against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), section 3.5, p. 3-37, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Install barriers to mitigate high-speed avenues of approach, deny vehicle entry, and provide perimeter protection. Options include, but are not limited to, fixed and retractable bollards, engineered planters, heavy objects and trees, walls and ha-ha barriers, water obstacles, and Jersey barriers.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings, (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Install fixed bollards, engineered planters, and/or heavy objects to mitigate high-speed avenues of approach.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Install a bollard system that is well-integrated into the perimeter security design and the streetscape to minimize visual impact. Bollard height should typically not be more than 30 inches. Bollard spacing should be between 36 and 43 inches, depending on the kind of traffic expected and the needs of pedestrians and the handicapped.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Install planters with considerable reinforcing and below-grade depth for effectiveness. Maintain a maximum distance of 4 feet between planters and other permanent streetscape elements, depending on the kind of traffic anticipated. Orient planters in a direction parallel to the curb or primary flow of pedestrian traffic. Do not place planter or a line of planters perpendicular to the curb.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Install heavy objects that can resist vehicle penetration, such as boulders, sculptures, and trees. Such objects need varying degrees of embedment and reinforcement, depending on their weight, footprint, and height/width ratio.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Explore the possibility of erecting walls, which includes retaining walls and freestanding walls, that may be constructed of concrete, concrete masonry, brick, natural stone, or other materials typically reinforced with steel.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Install Jersey barriers. To be effective, Jersey barriers need to be embedded and include vertical anchorage of steel reinforcing through the barrier into the pavement.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Install Jersey barriers as a temporary solution. Jersey barriers are relatively inexpensive and readily available.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Explore the possibility of adding speed controls (e.g., serpentines or speed bumps) to limit vehicle speed at impact.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security, Guidance Against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), Appendix A, p. A-5, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Explore the possibility of installing active barriers, such as retractable bollards or a wedge system, to mitigate high-speed avenues of approach. Active barriers must be tended at all times by trained personnel and require regular maintenance to ensure continued operation.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-56 to 2-61, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Explore the possibility of using CPTED principles, concepts, and strategies (e.g., water barriers, landscaping) to install barriers. Consult Appendix A of Site and Urban Design for Security: Guidance against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), available at www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, and Tables 2.4 and 2.5 of Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips- 06 pdf for more information.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014; FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security, Guidance Against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), Appendix A, p. A-1 to A-6, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Employ temporary passive barriers (e.g., Jersey barriers) to eliminate straight-line vehicular access to the facility as needed (e.g., under special circumstances or during elevated threat situations).
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 1.8.3, p. 1-40, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Use vehicles as temporary physical barriers to eliminate straight-line vehicular access to the facility during elevated threat conditions.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 1.8.3, p. 1-40, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-56 to 2-61, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Explore the possibility of using CPTED principles, concepts, and strategies (e.g., water barriers, landscaping) to install barriers. Consult Appendix A of Site and Urban Design for Security: Guidance against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), available at www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, and Tables 2.4 and 2.5 of Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips- 06 pdf for more information.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014; FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security, Guidance Against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), Appendix A, p. A-1 to A-6, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.-

options.option_text: Employ temporary passive barriers (e.g., Jersey barriers) to eliminate straight-line vehicular access to the facility as needed (e.g., under special circumstances or during elevated threat situations).
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 1.8.3, p. 1-40, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Use vehicles as temporary physical barriers to eliminate straight-line vehicular access to the facility during elevated threat conditions.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 1.8.3, p. 1-40, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have K-rated barriers. K-rated barriers can significantly reduce the risk of vehicle penetration.

options.option_text: Install K-rated barriers in areas vulnerable to invasive vehicles. Consult Site and Urban Design for Security: Guidance against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, for more information.
options.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Facilities Physical Security Measures Guideline (ASIS GDL FPSM-2009), Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, section 3.2.1.2., p. 13; FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.1.1, p. 2-14, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of upgrading all barriers to meet K-rated standards. Consult Site and Urban Design for Security: Guidance against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, for more information.
options.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Facilities Physical Security Measures Guideline (ASIS GDL FPSM-2009), Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, section 3.2.1.2., p. 13; FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.1.1, p. 2-14, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

vulnerability_text: Although the facility has K-rated barriers in some areas, it lacks K-rated barriers in other areas that are vulnerable to invasive vehicles. K-rated barriers can significantly reduce the risk of vehicle penetration.

options.option_text: Install K-rated barriers in areas vulnerable to invasive vehicles. Consult Site and Urban Design for Security: Guidance against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, for more information.
options.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Facilities Physical Security Measures Guideline (ASIS GDL FPSM-2009), Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, section 3.2.1.2., p. 13; FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.1.1, p. 2-14, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of upgrading all barriers to meet K-rated standards. Consult Site and Urban Design for Security: Guidance against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, for more information.
options.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Facilities Physical Security Measures Guideline (ASIS GDL FPSM-2009), Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, section 3.2.1.2., p. 13; FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.1.1, p. 2-14, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not use barriers to enforce an effective standoff distance, i.e., the distance between the facility and the potential location for an explosive detonation.

options.option_text: Install barriers to increase standoff distance and reduce damage from a potential explosive device. Options include, but are not limited to, fixed and retractable bollards, engineered planters, heavy objects and trees, walls and ha-ha barriers, water obstacles, and Jersey barriers.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Install fixed bollards, engineered planters, and/or heavy objects, such as boulders and sculptures, to increase standoff distance from a potential explosive device and reduce damage.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Explore alternatives if it is not possible to create sufficient standoff distance through the use of barriers. Options may include hardening the building and minimizing hazardous flying debris during an explosive event (e.g., window protective measures such as anti-shatter film, bullet-resistant glass, and laminated glass).
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 3.3, p. 3-31 to 3-60, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Employ temporary passive barriers (e.g., Jersey barriers) to increase standoff distance as needed (e.g., under special circumstances or during elevated threat conditions).
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 1.8.3, p. 1-40, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Use vehicles as temporary physical barriers to increase standoff distance during elevated threat conditions.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 1.8.3 p. 1-40, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Install a bollard system that is well-integrated into the perimeter security design and the streetscape to minimize visual impact. Bollard height should typically not be more than 30 inches. Bollard spacing should be between 36 and 43 inches, depending on the kind of traffic expected and the needs of pedestrians and the handicapped.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Install planters with considerable reinforcing and below-grade depth for effectiveness. Maintain a maximum distance of 4 feet between planters and other permanent streetscape elements, depending on the kind of traffic anticipated. Orient planters in a direction parallel to the curb or primary flow of pedestrian traffic. Do not place planter or a line of planters perpendicular to the curb.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Install heavy objects that can resist vehicle penetration, such as boulders, sculptures, and trees. Such objects need varying degrees of embedment and reinforcement, depending on their weight, footprint, and height/width ratio.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Explore the possibility of erecting walls, which includes retaining walls and freestanding walls, that may be constructed of concrete, concrete masonry, brick, natural stone, or other materials typically reinforced with steel.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Install Jersey barriers. To be effective, Jersey barriers need to be embedded and include vertical anchorage of steel reinforcing through the barrier into the pavement.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Install Jersey barriers as a temporary solution. Jersey barriers are relatively inexpensive and readily available.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Explore the possibility of using CPTED principles, concepts, and strategies (e.g., water barriers, landscaping) to install barriers. Consult Appendix A of Site and Urban Design for Security: Guidance against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), available at www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, and Tables 2.4 and 2.5 of Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips- 06 pdf for more information.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014; FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security, Guidance Against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), Appendix A, p. A-1 to A-6, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

vulnerability_text: Ground-floor windows do not have protective measures to mitigate the hazardous effects of flying glass during an explosive event.

options.option_text: Conduct an assessment to determine additional appropriate, feasible mitigation measures to reduce the vulnerability and risk associated with flying glass during an explosive event. Options to consider include, but are not limited to, anti-shatter film, blast curtains, bullet-resistant glass, and laminated glass.
options.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 3.3.3, p. 3-20 to 3-30, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Apply anti-shatter film to existing glass to provide fragment retention and reduce the overall velocity of glass fragments at failure, especially near regularly occupied and mass-gathering areas. Anti-shatter film may mitigate the impact of wind-blown debris and seismic stress in addition to explosive force by preventing pieces of broken glass from becoming lethal projectiles.
options.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 3.3.3, p. 3-20 to 3-30, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Consult Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, for more information.

vulnerability_text: Ground-floor windows do not have protective measures to deter entry. If they are not hardened, standard windows may be the weak link in a structure's barrier protection. Most standard windows can be penetrated with hand tools in less than a minute.

options.option_text: Install protective coverings, metal grills, and/or steel mesh to harden windows against penetration.
options.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 3.3.3, p. 3-20 to 3-30, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Determine whether windows are required. If not, explore the feasibility of permanently changing to a solid wall with no glazing, which would enhance penetration delay and help protect personnel, property, and operations from blast effects.

vulnerability_text: Ground-floor windows have limited protective measures (e.g., wire-reinforced glass). Although wire-reinforced glass binds some fragments, it still ejects a considerable amount of sharp glass and metal fragments during a blast event.

options.option_text: Conduct an assessment to determine additional appropriate, feasible mitigation measures to reduce the vulnerability and risk associated with flying glass during an explosive event. Options to consider include, but are not limited to, anti-shatter film, blast curtains, bullet-resistant glass, and laminated glass.
options.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 3.3.3, p. 3-20 to 3-30, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Consult Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, for more information.

vulnerability_text: Ground-floor windows have limited protective measures (e.g., thermally-tempered glass). During a blast event, thermally-tempered glass initially may be propelled into cohesive clumps that only fragment into smaller pieces upon impact; in addition, the high velocity of the fragments constitutes a severe hazard.

options.option_text: Conduct an assessment to determine additional appropriate, feasible mitigation measures to reduce the vulnerability and risk associated with flying glass during an explosive event. Options to consider include, but are not limited to, anti-shatter film, blast curtains, bullet-resistant glass, and laminated glass.
options.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 3.3.3, p. 3-20 to 3-30, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Consult Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, for more information.

vulnerability_text: Glass doors at the facility provide minimal penetration delay and may be breached easily to gain unauthorized access.

options.option_text: Replace glass doors with solid doors without glass panels, if possible.

vulnerability_text: Glass doors at the facility lack protective measures to minimize fragmentation after an explosive event. Following such incidents, a high number of injuries can result from flying glass fragments.

options.option_text: Conduct an assessment to determine additional appropriate, feasible mitigation measures to reduce the vulnerability and risk associated with flying glass during an explosive event. Options to consider include, but are not limited to, anti-shatter film, blast curtains, bullet-resistant glass, and laminated glass.
options.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 3.3.3, p. 3-20 to 3-30, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Apply anti-shatter film to existing glass to provide fragment retention and reduce the overall velocity of glass fragments at failure, especially near regularly occupied and mass-gathering areas. Anti-shatter film may mitigate the impact of wind-blown debris and seismic stress in addition to explosive force by preventing pieces of broken glass from becoming lethal projectiles.
options.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 3.3.3, p. 3-20 to 3-30, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Consult Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, for more information.

vulnerability_text: Facility doors with exposed hinges/pins are at risk of tampering. Exterior door hinges may be removed to open a locked door to gain unauthorized access.

options.option_text: Relocate hinges on the interior, if possible, or provide concealed hinges to reduce the likelihood of tampering. Hinge pins could also be welded to hinge to preclude easy removal.
options.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 3.3.4, p. 3-31, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Secure hinges, hinge pins, and hasps against unauthorized removal by using spot welds or peened mounting bolts.

vulnerability_text: Doors at the facility do not have reinforced strike plates. Reinforced strike plates harden doors against break-ins.

options.option_text: Install reinforced strike plates on facility doors to deter unauthorized forced entry.

vulnerability_text: The exterior of the facility has primarily glass construction and therefore lacks protective measures that would minimize fragmentation during an explosive event. Following such incidents, a high number of injuries can result from flying glass fragments.

options.option_text: Conduct an assessment to determine additional appropriate, feasible mitigation measures to reduce the vulnerability and risk associated with flying glass during an explosive event. Options to consider include, but are not limited to, anti-shatter film, blast curtains, bullet-resistant glass, and laminated glass.
options.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 3.3.3, p. 3-20 to 3-30, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Apply anti-shatter film to existing glass to provide fragment retention and reduce the overall velocity of glass fragments at failure, especially near regularly occupied and mass-gathering areas. Anti-shatter film may mitigate the impact of wind-blown debris and seismic stress in addition to explosive force by preventing pieces of broken glass from becoming lethal projectiles.
options.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 3.3.3, p. 3-20 to 3-30, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Consult Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, for more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility has skylights and/or other openings in the ceiling/roof that do not have protective measures and could allow entry.

options.option_text: Install screens or grates to ceiling/roof openings to deter entry.
options.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 3.3.5, p. 3-33, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Install intrusion detection sensors to detect attempts to penetrate or enter the facility through these openings.
options.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 3.3.5, p. 3-33, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

vulnerability_text: Outside air intakes are accessible and susceptible to the introduction of CBR agents.

options.option_text: Install protective measures, such as screens, to protect against malicious acts. Screens will prevent objects from being tossed into the intakes from the ground; such screens should be sloped to allow thrown objects to roll or slide off the screen, away from intakes.
options.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 3.4, p. 3-35 to 3-44, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Elevate accessible wall-mounted or below-ground intakes by constructing a plenum chamber or external shafts over the intakes. An extension height of 12 feet will place intakes out of reach of individuals without some assistance.
options.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 3.4, p. 3-35 to 3-44, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Install physical security measures, such as fencing, CCTV cameras, and motion detectors around air intakes to facilitate monitoring by security personnel.
options.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 3.4, p. 3-35 to 3-44, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Deploy the security force to regularly patrol and inspect outside air intakes for signs of malevolent activity, especially during elevated threat levels.
Consult Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, for more information.

vulnerability_text: Outside air intakes are accessible and susceptible to the introduction of CBR agents.

options.option_text: Install protective measures, such as screens, to protect against malicious acts. Screens will prevent objects from being tossed into the intakes from the ground; such screens should be sloped to allow thrown objects to roll or slide off the screen, away from intakes.
options.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 3.4, p. 3-35 to 3-44, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Elevate accessible wall-mounted or below-ground intakes by constructing a plenum chamber or external shafts over the intakes. An extension height of 12 feet will place intakes out of reach of individuals without some assistance.
options.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 3.4, p. 3-35 to 3-44, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Install physical security measures, such as fencing, CCTV cameras, and motion detectors around air intakes to facilitate monitoring by security personnel.
options.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 3.4, p. 3-35 to 3-44, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Deploy the security force to regularly patrol and inspect outside air intakes for signs of malevolent activity, especially during elevated threat levels.
Consult Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, for more information.

vulnerability_text: The air-handling system does not include CBR detection capabilities to counter CBR threats.

options.option_text: Conduct an assessment to determine the most appropriate CBR detection system for the facility to protect against CBR threats. Consult Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, for more information.
Monitor CBR detection technologies, and carefully consider the costs and benefits of implementing such systems. If CBR detection technology is appropriate and approved for the facility:
options.reference: New York Police Department, 2009, Engineering Security: Protective Design for High Risk Buildings, ch. 7, http://www.nyc.gov/html/nypd/downloads/pdf/counterterrorism/nypd_engineeringsecurity_low_res.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Install CBR detectors in air ductwork systems and remote sensing systems in areas with large occupancy populations.
options.reference: New York Police Department, 2009, Engineering Security: Protective Design for High Risk Buildings, ch. 7, http://www.nyc.gov/html/nypd/downloads/pdf/counterterrorism/nypd_engineeringsecurity_low_res.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Place CBR detection technology on the building's exterior and interior.
options.reference: New York Police Department, 2009, Engineering Security: Protective Design for High Risk Buildings, ch. 7, http://www.nyc.gov/html/nypd/downloads/pdf/counterterrorism/nypd_engineeringsecurity_low_res.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Integrate CBR detection equipment into central security management control and building management systems so facility personnel are immediately notified of intentionally released hazards.
options.reference: New York Police Department, 2009, Engineering Security: Protective Design for High Risk Buildings, ch. 7, http://www.nyc.gov/html/nypd/downloads/pdf/counterterrorism/nypd_engineeringsecurity_low_res.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Inform local law enforcement and first responders of the facility's CBR countermeasures and associated emergency protocols.
options.reference: New York Police Department, 2009, Engineering Security: Protective Design for High Risk Buildings, ch. 7, http://www.nyc.gov/html/nypd/downloads/pdf/counterterrorism/nypd_engineeringsecurity_low_res.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The air-handling system does not include filters that can capture CBR agents and prevent their dissemination throughout the facility.

options.option_text: Conduct an assessment to determine the most appropriate filtration system for the facility to protect against CBR threats.
options.reference: New York Police Department, 2009, Engineering Security: Protective Design for High Risk Buildings, ch. 7, http://www.nyc.gov/html/nypd/downloads/pdf/counterterrorism/nypd_engineeringsecurity_low_res.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Invest in advanced filtration systems that can afford a measure of protection against CBR threats. Incorporate solid-particle filters with high minimum efficiency reporting value (MERV) ratings for maximum efficiency and effective filtration.
options.reference: New York Police Department, 2009, Engineering Security: Protective Design for High Risk Buildings, ch. 7, http://www.nyc.gov/html/nypd/downloads/pdf/counterterrorism/nypd_engineeringsecurity_low_res.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Consult Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, for more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility has access ports to SAAs other than a building (e.g., hatches to bridge gear boxes, hatches to under bridge structural components, or secreted doors/hatches to outdoor concert stages) that do not have protective measures, leaving the facility at risk of unauthorized access and covert entry.

options.option_text: Ensure that access ports are locked and secured.
options.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 3.11, p. 3-51 to 3-52, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Add monitoring and detection capabilities (e.g., CCTV cameras, IDS) to detect attempts to penetrate or enter the facility through these access ports.
options.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 3.11, p. 3-51 to 3-52, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Conduct frequent security checks of these access ports.
Permanently remove or block access to and from these access ports, if possible.

vulnerability_text: The facility sits above an underground area not within its control (e.g., utility tunnel, pedestrian tunnel, subway tunnel), which can be used to access the facility. There are no protective measures or monitoring capabilities in place for the access point(s) from this area to the facility.

options.option_text: Ensure that access point(s) are continuously controlled by the security force or locked when not guarded.
Add intrusion detection and assessment capabilities (e.g., CCTV cameras, IDS) to detect unauthorized attempts to enter the facility through the access point(s).
options.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 3.11, p. 3-51 to 3-52, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Conduct frequent security checks of the access point(s) to the facility from such areas.
Permanently remove or block access to and from underground areas, if possible.

vulnerability_text: The facility has multiple communications service connections, but they enter at the same geographic location, causing a potential single point of failure for facility communications.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of separating the communications service connections so they enter the facility in different locations. This will reduce the likelihood that an incident in one location would disrupt all facility communications systems.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), Appendix F, p. F-38 to F-39, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: Communications service connections do not terminate in a protected facility/building, which increases their risk to tampering and accidental or intentional damage.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of implementing protective measures to the facility/building in which communications service connections terminate.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), Appendix F, p. F-38 to F-39, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Secure terminal points for communications service connections against unauthorized access, for example, with door locks and/or card readers.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), Appendix F, p. F-38 to F-39, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Evaluate the need to implement enhanced protective measures for terminal points to communications service connections, such as intrusion detection sensors and CCTV.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), Appendix F, p. F-38 to F-39, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Conduct random security checks of critical interior utility closets and assets.

vulnerability_text: Communications service connections for the facility are located in a joint, co-located utility corridor; therefore, an incident in one location could disrupt all communications for the facility.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of relocating the communications service connections to help mitigate the risk that the catastrophic failure of one utility could inadvertently take down another.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), Appendix F, p. F-38 to F-39, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have a contingency/business continuity plan with the communications service provider for rapid restoration in the event of a service disruption.

options.option_text: Determine the communications service requirements to support facility core operations, and develop commensurate plans in collaboration with the provider to ensure service is restored with priority, or alternate service provisions are made. Ensure plans address legitimate hypothetical scenarios that would result in service loss and corresponding restoration timelines so the facility can address business continuity and interim workarounds.
options.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not participate in a plan with the communications service provider for priority restoration in the event of a service disruption.

options.option_text: Collaborate with the service provider to determine if the company prioritizes certain types of facilities for restoration before other customers in the event of a service disruption, and if the facility is eligible for priority restoration.
options.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: If the facility is not eligible for priority restoration before other customers, establish a contingency/business continuity plan to resume facility operations after a service disruption. As part of the planning process, collaborate with the utility provider to review options for rapid restoration of service. Establish a liaison with the utility provider to maintain communications during a service outage. Ensure the contingency/business continuity plan is well understood by facility management and employees.
options.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The chemical most critical to facility core operations is not available from multiple suppliers. In the event that the provider is unable to provide this chemical, facility operations may be severely impacted.

options.option_text: Collaborate with the provider to become better informed about the company's business continuity management program, notification procedures for supply disruptions, and the overall resilience of the supply system. Use this information to improve the facility's own business continuity plans, policies, and procedures, as necessary.

Evaluate the need to store this critical chemical onsite to mitigate the impacts of a disruption in deliveries from the primary provider. As part of the evaluation, consider potential requirements associated with storage of hazardous chemicals subject to Federal security regulations.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have a contingency/business continuity plan with the provider(s) of the chemical most critical to facility core operations for rapid restoration of deliveries in the event of a supply disruption.

options.option_text: Determine the supply requirements to support facility core operations, and develop commensurate plans in collaboration with the provider(s) to ensure delivery is restored with priority, or alternate provisions are made. Ensure plans address legitimate hypothetical scenarios that would result in a supply chain disruption and corresponding timelines so the facility can address business continuity and interim workarounds.
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management - Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.7, p. 24; Protiviti, 2006, Guide To Business Continuity Management Frequently Asked Questions, 2nd Edition, p. 40-46, http://www.protiviti.com/en-US/Documents/Resource-Guides/bcm_faq_booklet.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Provide adequate lead time or use automated ordering processes to maintain adequate supplies of this critical chemical.
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management - Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.7, p. 24; Protiviti, 2006, Guide To Business Continuity Management Frequently Asked Questions, 2nd Edition, p. 40-46, http://www.protiviti.com/en-US/Documents/Resource-Guides/bcm_faq_booklet.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Evaluate the need to store this critical chemical onsite to mitigate the impacts of a disruption in deliveries from the primary provider. As part of the evaluation, consider potential requirements associated with storage of hazardous chemicals subject to Federal security regulations.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not participate in a plan with the provider(s) of the chemical most critical to facility core operations for priority restoration of deliveries in the event of a supply disruption.

options.option_text: Collaborate with the provider(s) to determine if they prioritize certain types of customers for restoration of deliveries before other customers in the event of a supply disruption, and if the facility is eligible for priority restoration.

If the facility is not eligible for priority restoration of deliveries before other customers, establish a contingency/business continuity plan to resume facility operations after a supply disruption. As part of the planning process, collaborate with the provider(s) to review options for rapid restoration of deliveries. Establish a liaison with the provider(s) to maintain communications during a supply disruption. Ensure the contingency/business continuity plan is well understood by facility management and employees.

vulnerability_text: The facility has no alternate (e.g., onsite storage) to the delivery of the chemical most critical to facility core operations. In the event that deliveries cannot be made, facility operations may be significantly impacted.

options.option_text: Evaluate the need to store this critical chemical onsite to mitigate the impacts of a disruption in deliveries from the primary provider. As part of the evaluation, consider potential requirements associated with storage of hazardous chemicals subject to Federal security regulations.

vulnerability_text: The fuel most critical to facility core operations is not available from multiple suppliers. In the event that the provider is unable to provide this fuel, facility operations may be severely impacted.

options.option_text: Collaborate with the provider to become better informed about the company's business continuity management program, notification procedures for supply disruptions, and the overall resilience of the supply system. Use this information to improve the facility's own business continuity plans, policies, and procedures, as necessary.
Explore the feasibility of storing this critical fuel onsite to mitigate the impacts of a disruption in supply.
Explore the feasibility of increasing onsite storage of this critical fuel to mitigate the impacts of a disruption in deliveries from the provider.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have a contingency/business continuity plan with the provider(s) of the fuel most critical to facility core operations for rapid restoration of deliveries in the event of a supply disruption.

options.option_text: Determine the supply requirements to support facility core operations, and develop commensurate plans in collaboration with the provider(s) to ensure delivery is restored with priority, or alternate provisions are made. Ensure plans address legitimate hypothetical scenarios that would result in a supply chain disruption and corresponding timelines so the facility can address business continuity and interim workarounds.
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management - Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.7, p. 24; Protiviti, 2006, Guide To Business Continuity Management Frequently Asked Questions, 2nd Edition, p. 40-46, http://www.protiviti.com/en-US/Documents/Resource-Guides/bcm_faq_booklet.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Provide adequate lead time or use automated ordering processes to maintain adequate supplies of this critical fuel.
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management - Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.7, p. 24; Protiviti, 2006, Guide To Business Continuity Management Frequently Asked Questions, 2nd Edition, p. 40-46, http://www.protiviti.com/en-US/Documents/Resource-Guides/bcm_faq_booklet.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of storing this critical fuel onsite to mitigate the impacts of a disruption in supply.
Explore the feasibility of increasing onsite storage of this critical fuel to mitigate the impacts of a disruption in deliveries from the provider.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not participate in a plan with the provider(s) of the fuel most critical to facility core operations for priority restoration of deliveries in the event of a supply disruption.

options.option_text: Collaborate with the provider(s) to determine if they prioritize certain types of customers for restoration of deliveries before other customers in the event of a supply disruption, and if the facility is eligible for priority restoration.

If the facility is not eligible for priority restoration of deliveries before other customers, establish a contingency/business continuity plan to resume facility operations after a supply disruption. As part of the planning process, collaborate with the provider(s) to review options for rapid restoration of deliveries. Establish a liaison with the provider(s) to maintain communications during a supply disruption. Ensure the contingency/business continuity plan is well understood by facility management and employees.

vulnerability_text: The facility has no alternate (e.g., onsite storage) to the delivery of the fuel most critical to facility core operations. In the event that deliveries cannot be made, facility operations may be significantly impacted.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of storing this critical fuel onsite to mitigate the impacts of a disruption in supply.

vulnerability_text: Byproduct/waste removal service is critical to facility core operations but is not available from multiple service providers. In the event that the service provider is unable to remove byproducts/waste from the site, facility operations may be severely impacted.

options.option_text: Collaborate with the service provider to become better informed about the company's business continuity management program, notification procedures for service disruptions, and the overall resilience of the service system. Use this information to improve the facility's own business continuity plans, policies, and procedures, as necessary.
Evaluate the need to provide onsite storage capacity for byproducts/waste to mitigate the impacts of a disruption in removal service.
Explore the feasibility of increasing onsite storage capacity for byproducts/waste to mitigate the impacts of a disruption in removal service.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have a contingency/business continuity plan with the byproduct/waste removal service provider(s) for rapid restoration of service in the event of a disruption.

options.option_text: Determine the service requirements to support facility core operations, and develop commensurate plans in collaboration with the provider(s) to ensure service is restored with priority, or alternate provisions are made. Ensure plans address legitimate hypothetical scenarios that would result in a service disruption and corresponding timelines so the facility can address business continuity and interim workarounds.
Evaluate the need to provide onsite storage capacity for byproducts/waste to mitigate the impacts of a disruption in removal service.
Explore the feasibility of increasing onsite storage capacity for byproducts/waste to mitigate the impacts of a disruption in removal service.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not participate in a plan with the byproduct/waste removal service provider(s) for priority restoration of service in the event of a disruption.

options.option_text: Collaborate with the provider(s) to determine if they prioritize certain types of customers for restoration of service before other customers in the event of a disruption, and if the facility is eligible for priority restoration.

If the facility is not eligible for priority restoration of service before other customers, establish a contingency/business continuity plan to resume facility operations after a service disruption. As part of the planning process, collaborate with the provider(s) to review options for rapid restoration of service. Establish a liaison with the provider(s) to maintain communications during a service disruption. Ensure the contingency/business continuity plan is well understood by facility management and employees.

vulnerability_text: The facility has no alternate (e.g., onsite storage) to the removal of byproducts/waste by the service provider(s). In the event that byproducts/waste cannot be removed from the site, facility operations may be severely impacted.

options.option_text: Evaluate the need to provide onsite storage capacity for byproducts/waste to mitigate the impacts of a disruption in removal service.

vulnerability_text: The raw material most critical to facility core operations is not available from multiple suppliers. In the event that the provider is unable to provide this material, facility operations may be severely impacted.

options.option_text: Collaborate with the provider to become better informed about the company's business continuity management program, notification procedures for supply disruptions, and the overall resilience of the supply system. Use this information to improve the facility's own business continuity plans, policies, and procedures, as necessary.
Explore the feasibility of storing this critical material onsite to mitigate the impacts of a disruption in supply.
Explore the feasibility of increasing onsite storage of this critical material to mitigate the impacts of a disruption in deliveries from the provider.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have a contingency/business continuity plan with the provider(s) of the raw material most critical to facility core operations for rapid restoration of deliveries in the event of a supply disruption.

options.option_text: Determine the supply requirements to support facility core operations, and develop commensurate plans in collaboration with the provider(s) to ensure delivery is restored with priority, or alternate provisions are made. Ensure plans address legitimate hypothetical scenarios that would result in a supply chain disruption and corresponding timelines so the facility can address business continuity and interim workarounds.

options.option_text: Provide adequate lead time or use automated ordering processes to maintain adequate supplies of this critical material.

Explore the feasibility of increasing onsite storage of this critical material to mitigate the impacts of a disruption in deliveries from the provider.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not participate in a plan with the provider(s) of the raw material most critical to facility core operations for priority restoration of deliveries in the event of a supply disruption.

options.option_text: Collaborate with the provider(s) to determine if they prioritize certain types of customers for restoration of deliveries before other customers in the event of a supply disruption, and if the facility is eligible for priority restoration.

If the facility is not eligible for priority restoration of deliveries before other customers, establish a contingency/business continuity plan to resume facility operations after a supply disruption. As part of the planning process, collaborate with the provider(s) to review options for rapid restoration of deliveries. Establish a liaison with the provider(s) to maintain communications during a supply disruption. Ensure the contingency/business continuity plan is well understood by facility management and employees.

vulnerability_text: The facility has no alternate (e.g., onsite storage) to the delivery of the raw material most critical to facility core operations. In the event that deliveries cannot be made, facility operations may be significantly impacted.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of storing this critical material onsite to mitigate the impacts of a disruption in supply.

vulnerability_text: At the time of the visit, the substation(s) servicing the facility was unknown. This lack of knowledge may be indicative of a lack of communication between the facility and the electric power utility provider.

options.option_text: Reach out to the utility provider to determine the substation(s) that services the facility. Maintaining regular communication channels with utility service providers will enhance the facility's understanding of actions to be taken in response to a loss of service from primary supply sources, restoration priorities, and potential alternatives.
options.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility has only one electric service connection, creating a potential single point of failure in its electric power supply.

options.option_text: Collaborate with the electric power utility provider on the feasibility of installing a second service connection that enters the facility in a different location to create redundancy. It is preferred that a secondary connection be serviced from a different substation and that the connection be able to support the entire load of the facility.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87 to 3-89, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: Neither electric service connection for the facility is capable of handling the entire facility load. Therefore, if service from one connection was disrupted, the electric power supply to the facility would be degraded.

options.option_text: Install an emergency generator capable of providing adequate power for the entire facility load during an extended commercial power outage.
Collaborate with electric power utility provider to explore options to modify service connections such that any one connection could meet the entire facility load.

vulnerability_text: All electric service connections for the facility enter at the same geographic location, causing a potential single point of failure in the electric power supply for the facility.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of separating the electric service connections so they enter the facility at different locations.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87 to 3-89, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The electric power service connection(s) for the facility is collocated with other utilities (e.g., in utility corridors for natural gas, communications, fiber, water): therefore, an incident in one location could disrupt the operations of multiple service systems.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of relocating the electric service connection(s) to help mitigate the risk that the catastrophic failure of one utility could inadvertently take down another.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87 to 3-89, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: There are no protective measures in place for electrical system components inside the facility building(s), leaving them at risk for tampering and accidental or intentional damage.

options.option_text: Secure components of the electrical system inside the facility against unauthorized access, for example, with access-controlled utility closets and locked electrical boxes.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87 to 3-89, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Evaluate the need to implement enhanced protective measures for electrical system components, such as intrusion detection sensors and CCTV.
Conduct random security checks of critical interior utility closets and assets.

vulnerability_text: There are no protective measures in place for electrical system components outside the facility building(s), leaving them at risk for tampering and accidental or intentional damage.

options.option_text: Secure components of the electrical system outside of the facility against unauthorized access, for example, with door locks.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87 to 3-89, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Install a fence or protective structure around exterior electrical system equipment (e.g., transformers, switchgear) to secure them against unauthorized access and to decrease the risk of accidental or intentional damage to these critical components.
options.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 2.4.1, p. 2-27 to 2-30, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Evaluate the need to implement enhanced protective measures for components of the electrical system, such as intrusion detection sensors and CCTV.
Conduct random security checks of critical exterior electrical system equipment.

vulnerability_text: Electrical system components are unprotected from accidental or intentional vehicle damage.

options.option_text: Install vehicle barriers to protect electric power equipment from damage.
Explore the feasibility of prohibiting parking near electric power equipment. Utility systems should be located at least 50 feet from loading docks, front entrances, and parking areas.
options.reference: FEMA, 2005, Risk Management Series – Risk Assessment: A How-To Guide to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 452), Appendix A, p. A-11, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1524-20490-7395/fema452_01_05.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have a contingency/business continuity plan with the electric power utility provider for rapid restoration in the event of a service disruption.

options.option_text: Determine the electric power requirements to support facility core operations, and develop commensurate plans in collaboration with the utility provider to ensure service is restored with priority, or alternate service provisions are made. Ensure plans address legitimate hypothetical scenarios that would result in service loss and corresponding restoration timelines so the facility can address business continuity and interim workarounds.
options.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not participate in a plan with the electric power utility provider for priority restoration in the event of a service disruption.

options.option_text: Collaborate with the utility provider to determine if the utility prioritizes certain types of facilities for restoration before other customers in the event of a service disruption, and if the facility is eligible for priority restoration.
options.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: If the facility is not eligible for priority restoration before other customers, establish a contingency/business continuity plan to resume facility operations after a service disruption. As part of the planning process, collaborate with the utility provider to review options for rapid restoration of service. Establish a liaison with the utility provider to maintain communications during a service outage. Ensure the contingency/business continuity plan is well understood by facility management and employees.
options.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have an alternate or backup that can be used in the event of a loss of commercial power. A loss of electric power could significantly disrupt facility operations.

options.option_text: Evaluate power requirements for the facility to maintain core operations, and install backup power equipment accordingly. At a minimum, ensure all life safety services have backup power.

Conduct an assessment to determine which systems and equipment are essential to the continued operation of the facility. They may include one or more of the following: HVAC systems; industrial equipment and major appliances; lights (interior and exterior), computers, and other office equipment; pumps, including sump pumps, sprinkler system pumps, and well water pumps; and alarm systems. Once essential systems and equipment have been identified, determine how much power they require, and then consult with a generator technical expert regarding the appropriate size and type of generator(s) for the facility. The technical expert can also help select other components of the emergency power system, including the main transfer switch and the electrical panel.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Install a Generator for Emergency Power, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Explore options to enter into a contract to have a backup power generator readily available, if installing a generator onsite is not feasible. Ensure that the facility is equipped (i.e., has the necessary wiring and transfer switches) to facilitate the rapid installation and operation of the generator unit.

If the installation of three or more backup power generators is planned, consider installing them in two separate locations to mitigate the vulnerability of a single point of failure. Similarly, the electric utility switchgear, the automatic transfer switch, and the backup switchgear should be in separate locations with sufficient separation distance.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.2.2, p. 3-26, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Consult the FEMA flyer Install a Generator for Emergency Power, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf, for more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility has no contracts or procedures in place for refueling during emergencies.

options.option_text: Develop contracts/procedures for refueling the backup power generator during emergencies. Consider the possibility that the primary supplier may not be available, and establish contracts with alternate fuel providers, if possible.
options.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. 4-11, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The backup power generator provides power only for life safety services such as emergency lighting: therefore, a loss of commercial electric power would significantly disrupt facility operations.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of increasing the capacity of the backup power system to support at least core operations and life safety services when the primary power supply is disrupted.
Upgrade the backup power system so security systems such as IDS and CCTV may also receive emergency power in the event of a commercial power outage.

vulnerability_text: The backup power generator provides power only for graceful shutdown of the facility: therefore, a loss of commercial electric power would significantly disrupt facility operations.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of increasing the capacity of the backup power system to support at least core operations and life safety services when the primary power supply is disrupted.
Upgrade the backup power system so security systems such as IDS and CCTV may also receive emergency power in the event of a commercial power outage.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not routinely test the backup power generator under load.

options.option_text: Develop routine load test policies and procedures for emergency power generators. Use manufacturer’s recommendations and industry standards to determine frequency, duration, and percent of load for tests.
Develop a plan to maintain and test the backup power generator regularly.

vulnerability_text: Although the facility routinely tests the backup power generator under load, it does so on a quarterly basis. More frequent testing of the backup power generator may help to ensure it will operate as expected during an emergency.

options.option_text: Review the manufacturer’s recommendations and industry standards to determine if the backup power generator requires more frequent testing.

vulnerability_text: Although the facility routinely tests the backup power generator under load, it does so on a semi-annual basis. More frequent testing of the backup power generator may help to ensure it will operate as expected during an emergency.

options.option_text: Review the manufacturer’s recommendations and industry standards to determine if the backup power generator requires more frequent testing.

vulnerability_text: Although the facility routinely tests the backup power generator under load, it does so on an annual basis. More frequent testing of the backup power generator may help to ensure it will operate as expected during an emergency.

options.option_text: Review the manufacturer’s recommendations and industry standards to determine if the backup power generator requires more frequent testing.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have a designated manager or department in charge of IT security. The lack of a designated IT security manager/department may hinder the implementation of IT security policies, programs, or procedures.

options.option_text: Appoint or nominate a person with the appropriate training, knowledge, and authority to be accountable for IT security policy and implementation.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 21-25, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not report cybersecurity incidents to outside organizations. Information sharing with other entities increases cyber resilience.

options.option_text: Report all cyber incidents to appropriate outside organizations. Cyber incident reports can be made to request technical assistance, to request incident management support, to inform law enforcement, and/or for information sharing.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 33, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Report all cyber incidents to appropriate outside organizations. Cyber incident reports can be made to request technical assistance, request incident management support, and/or for information sharing. Information sharing plays a key role in the prevention of cyber attacks and ensures new threats can be identified quickly and effective countermeasures can be developed to neutralize/defeat them. Cyber threats/attack techniques are continuously evolving and change on a daily basis; many organizations/companies may be unaware of these latest threats. Participating in information sharing with other outside organizations and facilities may reduce this vulnerability and could possibly prevent a future cybersecurity attack from being successful.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 33, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

vulnerability_text: No one from the facility actively participates in local or regional cybersecurity forums (e.g., exchange lessons learned and/or best practices, training). Participation in cybersecurity forums increases cyber resilience.

options.option_text: Ensure facility IT representatives actively participate in cybersecurity forums. Examples of such forums include sector-specific information sharing and analysis centers, sector-related associations/partnerships, Federal or State-led partnerships (e.g., FBI InfraGard chapters), fusion centers, State or local law enforcement departments, and State or local IT offices.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 22, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.
