vulnerability_text: The facility does not receive threat and vulnerability information, cybersecurity-related bulletins, advisories, or alerts from an external source.

options.option_text: Establish regular communication channels with external sources of cyber security information. Available sources include DHS US-CERT, DHS ICS-CERT, the DHS Open Source Enterprise Daily Cyber Report, HSIN, and SANS Internet Storm Center.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 22, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not utilize formal, external cybersecurity guidance and standards for identifying and implementing cybersecurity controls. Not making use of established guidance and standards may increase the facility's risk to cyber attack and decrease the facility's overall cyber resilience.

options.option_text: Tailor cybersecurity policies and configurations for the facility's IT network in accordance with established cybersecurity guidance and standards such as NIST Special Publications 800-series, ISO/IEC 27001, COBIT, and ITIL.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 1-2, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not perform threat monitoring or threat management/remediation. A lack of active monitoring and threat management/remediation increases the facility's risk to cyber attack and decrease the facility's overall cyber resilience.

options.option_text: Engage in active threat monitoring and threat management/remediation activities.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 22, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Hire a third-party to provide cyber threat and vulnerability information as well as real-time system monitoring services.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 22, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have an IT service provider or an internal cyber team that is responsible for responding to, coordinating, or managing cyber incidents, which decreases its overall cyber resilience.

options.option_text: Employ an IT service provider or appoint an internal team to respond immediately to cyber incidents. If an internal team will be responsible for cyber incident response, ensure the team has the training, resources, and equipment necessary to effectively respond.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 20-22, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NIST, 2010, "Background," NIST SP 800-34 Revision 1: Contingency Planning Guide for Federal Information Systems, May, http://csrc.nist.gov/publications/nistpubs/800-34-rev1/sp800-34-rev1_errata-Nov11-2010.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have a written cybersecurity plan.

options.option_text: Develop a detailed cybersecurity plan that identifies management, employee, and contractor IT and cybersecurity responsibilities.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 21-22, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NIST, 2010, "Background," NIST SP 800-34 Revision 1: Contingency Planning Guide for Federal Information Systems, May, http://csrc.nist.gov/publications/nistpubs/800-34-rev1/sp800-34-rev1_errata-Nov11-2010.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Ensure the cybersecurity plan addresses asset management, information and technology management, vulnerability management, incident management, service continuity, environmental control, external dependency management, and situational awareness.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 21-22, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NIST, 2010, "Background," NIST SP 800-34 Revision 1: Contingency Planning Guide for Federal Information Systems, May, http://csrc.nist.gov/publications/nistpubs/800-34-rev1/sp800-34-rev1_errata-Nov11-2010.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Establish an assessment program to ensure the cybersecurity plan has been implemented effectively.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 21-22, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NIST, 2010, "Background," NIST SP 800-34 Revision 1: Contingency Planning Guide for Federal Information Systems, May, http://csrc.nist.gov/publications/nistpubs/800-34-rev1/sp800-34-rev1_errata-Nov11-2010.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Consult Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, available at http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, for more information about creating a move comprehensive cybersecurity program.

vulnerability_text: Senior management personnel have not approved the facility's cybersecurity plan, which suggests a possible lack of buy-in or oversight of cybersecurity concerns.

options.option_text: Acquire the signature and approval of a senior manager or designated representative for the cybersecurity plan.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 24-25, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NIST, 2010, "Background," NIST SP 800-34 Revision 1: Contingency Planning Guide for Federal Information Systems, May, http://csrc.nist.gov/publications/nistpubs/800-34-rev1/sp800-34-rev1_errata-Nov11-2010.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility's cybersecurity plan is not reviewed annually. Without regular reviews, a cybersecurity strategy will become outdated and its effectiveness will decrease.

options.option_text: Review the cybersecurity plan at least annually to determine if changes have taken place or new cyber issues have surfaced that the current plan does not address. As part of this process, identify the need for new policies, procedures, training/education, and security controls that should be added to the cybersecurity plan.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 26-28, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NIST, 2010, "Background," NIST SP 800-34 Revision 1: Contingency Planning Guide for Federal Information Systems, May, http://csrc.nist.gov/publications/nistpubs/800-34-rev1/sp800-34-rev1_errata-Nov11-2010.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

vulnerability_text: Key personnel are not aware of, or do not have access to a copy of, the cybersecurity plan.

options.option_text: Ensure key personnel are aware of, and have access to a copy of, the cybersecurity plan.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 20, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Ensure that key personnel are aware of the cybersecurity plan, and understand their contributions to its effectiveness, the implications of not conforming, and their roles and responsibilities during cybersecurity incidents.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 20, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not train personnel on the cybersecurity plan.

options.option_text: Implement a recurring cybersecurity training program to increase cybersecurity awareness, and to inform personnel on recognizing and responding appropriately to cybersecurity threats and incidents.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 24-25, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Provide initial and annual recurring cybersecurity training to all personnel prior to allowing network access. Document the completion of training.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 24-25, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility trains some, but not all, personnel on the cybersecurity plan.

options.option_text: Provide initial and annual recurring cybersecurity training to all personnel prior to allowing network access. Document the completion of training.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 24-25, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

vulnerability_text:
The facility's cybersecurity plan is missing key elements. 
Update the cybersecurity plan to include the following:

options.option_text: Update the cybersecurity plan to include the following:
Identification and classification of cyber critical assets: Identify the mission- or business-critical functions and identify the resources that support the critical functions.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 20, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NIST, 1995, "Preparing for Contingencies and Disasters," NIST SP 800-12: An Introduction to Computer Security - The NIST Handbook, October, http://csrc.nist.gov/publications/nistpubs/800-12/800-12-html/chapter11.html, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Access control policies: access control is necessary to ensure that use of the system or information is provided only to authorized users who have identified and authenticated themselves to obtain access. Consult NIST Interagency Report 7316: Assessment of Access Control Systems, available at http://csrc.nist.gov/publications/nistir/7316/NISTIR-7316.pdf, for more information.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 23-24, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NIST, 2014, "Role Based Access Control (RBAC) and Role Based Security," http://csrc.nist.gov/groups/SNS/rbac/, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: IT security roles and responsibilities: Defining clear roles and responsibilities is necessary to ensure that cyber security is properly managed and executed, that all necessary duties are assigned, that personnel receive the proper training, and that personnel have only the privileges necessary to fulfill their assignments.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NIST, 1995, "Roles & Responsibilities," NIST SP 800-12: An Introduction to Computer Security - The NIST Handbook, October, http://csrc.nist.gov/publications/nistpubs/800-12/800-12-html/chapter3.html, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: IT security training: Personnel need sufficient training, based upon their roles and responsibilities, to ensure they can perform their jobs without introducing security vulnerabilities. Consult NIST SP 800-53 Revision 4: Security and Privacy Controls for Federal Information Systems and Organizations, available at http://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/SpecialPublications/NIST.SP.800-53r4.pdf, for more information.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 24-25, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Audit trails: Without an audit trail, an organization might fail to detect security compromises, misuse of information, or other security-related events. Consult NIST SP 800-53 Revision 4: Security and Privacy Controls for Federal Information Systems and Organizations, available at http://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/SpecialPublications/NIST.SP.800-53r4.pdf, for more information.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 29, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Disposal of protected assets: Sensitive information must be removed using proper sanitation methods, or media destruction, to ensure information is not released during disposal of protected assets. Consult NIST SP 800-53 Revision 4: Security and Privacy Controls for Federal Information Systems and Organizations, available at http://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/SpecialPublications/NIST.SP.800-53r4.pdf, for more information.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 26-28, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NIST, 2013, NIST SP 800-53 Revision 4: Security and Privacy Controls for Federal Information Systems and Organizations, April, http://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/SpecialPublications/NIST.SP.800-53r4.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Incident response/management: This element captures the means of the facility to detect and respond to five categories of cyber incidents: Unauthorized Access, DOS, Malicious Code, Improper Usage, and Scans/Probes/Attempted Access. The incidents listed can be detected by multiple technical means including intrusion detection/prevention systems, firewalls, anti-virus tools, and vulnerability detection/assessment tools. Managing responses to incidents is necessary to ensure that incidents are properly detected and reported so that the incident is handled properly, forensic evidence is not lost, and essential operations are recovered as soon as possible.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 26-28, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Unauthorized access, i.e., an individual gains logical or physical access without permission to a network, system, application, data, or other resource. Unauthorized access may result in release of sensitive information or disruption of business operations.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 23-24, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: DOS, i.e., an attack that successfully prevents or impairs the normal authorized functionality of networks, systems, or applications by exhausting resources. This activity includes being the victim or participating in the DOS. Defending against DOS attacks can benefit from detecting the presence of an attack and receiving external support to block incoming network traffic.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 25-29, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Malicious code, i.e., the successful installation of malicious software (e.g., virus, worm, Trojan horse, or other code-based malicious entity) that infects an operating system or application.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 30-31, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Improper usage, i.e., a person violates acceptable computing use policies. Improper usage can result in the leak of sensitive information, the introduction of malicious code, the loss of system or information integrity, or other security-related events.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 30-31, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Scan/probes/attempted access, i.e., any activity that seeks to access or identify a computer, open parts, protocols, service, or any combination for later exploit. Use intrusion detection and prevention software or network behavior analysis software to detect anomalous external network traffic, such as scanning. Participate in regional information sharing to receive information about active scanning or network-based security attacks.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 30-31, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Unauthorized access, i.e., an individual gains logical or physical access without permission to a network, system, application, data, or other resource. Unauthorized access may result in release of sensitive information or disruption of business operations.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 23-24, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: DOS, i.e., an attack that successfully prevents or impairs the normal authorized functionality of networks, systems, or applications by exhausting resources. This activity includes being the victim or participating in the DOS. Defending against DOS attacks can benefit from detecting the presence of an attack and receiving external support to block incoming network traffic.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 25-29, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Malicious code, i.e., the successful installation of malicious software (e.g., virus, worm, Trojan horse, or other code-based malicious entity) that infects an operating system or application.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 30-31, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Improper usage, i.e., a person violates acceptable computing use policies. Improper usage can result in the leak of sensitive information, the introduction of malicious code, the loss of system or information integrity, or other security-related events.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 30-31, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Scan/probes/attempted access, i.e., any activity that seeks to access or identify a computer, open parts, protocols, service, or any combination for later exploit. Use intrusion detection and prevention software or network behavior analysis software to detect anomalous external network traffic, such as scanning. Participate in regional information sharing to receive information about active scanning or network-based security attacks.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 30-31, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Security testing: Testing is necessary to verify and validate that the intended security rules are properly implemented. Vulnerabilities include software holes that a hacker might take advantage of, out of date virus definition files, default passwords set by a vendor, or accounts that are not password protected. Software designed for scanning, such as ISS or Nessus, is commonly used, as is active scan systems, which detect vulnerabilities as soon as a system joins the network.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 30-31, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Physical security of IT security assets: Without proper physical and environmental security, information systems are vulnerable to intentional attacks or accidents that can disruption their operations and release sensitive information.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 23-24, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NIST, 1995, "Physical and Environmental Security," NIST SP 800-12: An Introduction to Computer Security - The NIST Handbook, October, http://csrc.nist.gov/publications/nistpubs/800-12/800-12-html/chapter15.html, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text:
Firewalls: Firewalls are devices or programs that control the flow of network traffic between networks or hosts that employ differing security postures. Without a firewall, internal systems are exposed to potential attacks against which they might not have sufficient protection. Consult NIST SP 800-41: Guidelines on Firewalls and 
Firewall Policy, available at http://csrc.nist.gov/publications/nistpubs/800-41-Rev1/sp800-41-rev1.pdf, for more information.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 29, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Electronic communications
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 29, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text:
Remote access: Remote access introduces numerous vulnerabilities ranging from lack of physical security on remote access devices (home computers, phones, tables), potential malware on those remote devices, information travels over unsecured networks (Internet), and opening internal resources to external devices. Securing remote access requires encrypting traffic from the remote devices, using technologies such as VPN, and strictly enforcing authentication. Consult NIST SP 800-46: Guide to Enterprise Telework 
and Remote Access Security, available at http://csrc.nist.gov/publications/nistpubs/800-46-rev1/sp800-46r1.pdf, for more information.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 23-24, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Wireless connectivity: Wireless technologies are typically less secure than wired technologies, primarily because they can extend outside of the physical footprint of the organization, but also because they often have weak security configurations. A best-case scenario would be for wireless to not be used at all, especially on control networks. Other scenarios may have a separate visitor network space, such that a visitor would not be able to scan traffic on the internal network. Employees would need to VPN or authenticate in some manner to gain access. A worst-case scenario would be that wireless is open to all, and exists on the same network as the internal systems. Consult NIST SP 800-153: Guidelines for Securing Wireless Local Area Networks (WLANs), available at http://csrc.nist.gov/publications/nistpubs/800-153/sp800-153.pdf, for more information on securing wireless technology.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 30-31, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Security patches or updates: A "patch" is a piece of computer code that a software company writes and distributes to fix a problem found in one of its previously released programs. Many patches fix problems related to security—specifically, vulnerabilities in the programs that attackers can exploit. Hackers seek out these vulnerabilities to gain access to a computer and its information, which then can be used to gain access to other vulnerable computers and information. These compromised computers also can be used to attack other computers. To prevent these problems, patches need to be deployed to computer systems quickly to minimize the window of opportunity for attackers. Consult NIST SP 800-40 Revision 3: Guide to Enterprise Patch Management Technologies, available at http://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/SpecialPublications/NIST.SP.800-40r3.pdf, for more information.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 30-31, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not conduct cybersecurity exercises for purposes of training, system testing, continuity planning, or disaster recovery. The periodic performance of exercises and testing helps to ensure the security protections perform as expected, or corrected if not, and personnel understand system and security controls.

options.option_text: Conduct regular cybersecurity exercises. An exercise is a simulation of an emergency designed to validate the viability of one or more aspects of an IT plan. In an exercise, personnel with roles and responsibilities in a particular IT plan meet to validate the content of a plan through discussion of their roles and their responses to emergency situations, execution of responses in a simulated operational environment, or other means of validating responses that does not involve using the actual operational environment. Exercises are scenario-driven, such as a power failure in one of the organization’s data centers or a fire causing certain systems to be damaged, with additional situations often being presented during the course of an exercise.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 26-28, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Document the results from cybersecurity exercises. Critically evaluate exercises to assess whether goals and objectives were achieved, the effectiveness of participation, and the adequacy of cybersecurity response policies and procedures in the event of a real cybersecurity attack. Modify cybersecurity plans, policies, and procedures as necessary.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 30-31, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Consult NIST SP 800-84: Guide to Test, Training, and Exercise Programs for IT Plans and Capabilities, available at http://csrc.nist.gov/publications/nistpubs/800-84/SP800-84.pdf, for more information.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 30-31, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility's cybersecurity plan is not exercised at least once a year.

options.option_text: Conduct at least annual exercises to validate the cybersecurity plan and to evaluate the ability of personnel to carry out their assigned roles and responsibilities.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 30-31, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NIST, 2010, "Background," NIST SP 800-34 Revision 1: Contingency Planning Guide for Federal Information Systems, May, http://csrc.nist.gov/publications/nistpubs/800-34-rev1/sp800-34-rev1_errata-Nov11-2010.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Document the results from exercises. Critically evaluate exercises to assess whether goals and objectives were achieved, the effectiveness of participation, and the adequacy of cybersecurity response policies and procedures in the event of a real cybersecurity attack. Modify the cybersecurity plan as necessary.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 30-31, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Consult NIST SP 800-84: Guide to Test, Training, and Exercise Programs for IT Plans and Capabilities, available at http://csrc.nist.gov/publications/nistpubs/800-84/SP800-84.pdf, for more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not document the results of cybersecurity plan exercises.

options.option_text: Document the results of cybersecurity plan exercises. Implement procedures for capturing and implementing lessons learned. Critically evaluate exercises to assess whether goals and objectives were achieved, the effectiveness of participation, and the adequacy of cybersecurity response policies and procedures in the event of a real cybersecurity attack. Modify the cybersecurity plan as necessary.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 30-31, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Consult NIST SP 800-84: Guide to Test, Training, and Exercise Programs for IT Plans and Capabilities, available at http://csrc.nist.gov/publications/nistpubs/800-84/SP800-84.pdf, for more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility has not had a cybersecurity assessment. Not identifying cybersecurity assets and vulnerabilities leaves the facility susceptible to cyber attacks and decreases its cyber resilience.

options.option_text: Conduct a cybersecurity assessment to ensure that the facility's IT systems are protected against cyber threats.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 22, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NIST, 2010, "Background," NIST SP 800-34 Revision 1: Contingency Planning Guide for Federal Information Systems, May, http://csrc.nist.gov/publications/nistpubs/800-34-rev1/sp800-34-rev1_errata-Nov11-2010.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Consult Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, available at http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, for more information about creating a move comprehensive cybersecurity program.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not perform security scans on the IT network, which may allow vulnerabilities and weaknesses in the network to remain undetected.

options.option_text: Implement the capability to scan the network for vulnerabilities and weaknesses. Implement a process for addressing the identified issues.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 30-31, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Implement the capability to scan the network for vulnerabilities and weaknesses. Implement a process for addressing the identified issues. Determine the frequency and comprehensiveness of vulnerability scans based on the security categorization of the facility's information systems, and ensure that potential sources of vulnerabilities such as networked printers, scanners, and copiers are not overlooked. Vulnerability analyses for custom software applications may require additional approaches such as static analysis, dynamic analysis, binary analysis, or a hybrid of the three approaches. Organizations can employ these analysis approaches in a variety of tools (e.g., Web-based application scanners, static analysis tools, binary analyzers) and in source code reviews.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 30-31, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NIST, 2013, NIST SP 800-53 Revision 4: Security and Privacy Controls for Federal Information Systems and Organization, p. F-153, April, http://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/SpecialPublications/NIST.SP.800-53r4.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Consult NIST SP 800-53 Revision 4: Security and Privacy Controls for Federal Information Systems and Organizations, available at http://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/SpecialPublications/NIST.SP.800-53r4.pdf, for more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility has no network segmentation between control networks and business networks. Network segmentation isolates critical assets from other less critical assets to minimize the reach of cyber attackers within the network.

options.option_text: Implement LAN segmentation physically or with VLANs. Use routing to provide systems cross-connectivity only where necessary. Employ access control measures to restrict access between segmented networks. Managed interfaces include, for example, gateways, routers, firewalls, guards, network-based malicious code analysis and virtualization systems, and encrypted tunnels implemented within a security architecture (e.g., routers protecting firewalls or application gateways residing on protected subnetworks). Restricting or prohibiting interfaces within organizational information systems includes, for example, restricting external Web traffic to designated Web servers within managed interfaces and prohibiting external traffic that appears to be spoofing internal addresses. Consider the shared nature of commercial telecommunications services in the implementation of security controls associated with the use of such services. Commercial telecommunications services are commonly based on network components and consolidated management systems shared by all attached commercial customers, and may also include third party-provided access lines and other service elements. Such transmission services may represent sources of increased risk despite contract security provisions.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 23-24, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NIST, 2013, NIST SP 800-53 Revision 4: Security and Privacy Controls for Federal Information Systems and Organizations, p. F-188, April, http://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/SpecialPublications/NIST.SP.800-53r4.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NIST, 2010, "Technical Contingency Planning Considerations," NIST SP 800-34 Revision 1: Contingency Planning Guide for Federal Information Systems, May, http://csrc.nist.gov/publications/nistpubs/800-34-rev1/sp800-34-rev1_errata-Nov11-2010.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Consult NIST SP 800-53 Revision 4: Security and Privacy Controls for Federal Information Systems and Organizations, available at http://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/SpecialPublications/NIST.SP.800-53r4.pdf, for more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have redundant, separated critical servers or network components.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of establishing redundant critical servers and/or network components preferably in different buildings or locations at a reasonably significant distance apart. This will reduce the likelihood that an incident in one location would disrupt the operations of these critical assets.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 29, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not use backup data storage, leaving it susceptible to the loss of critical and possibly irretrievable data and information.

options.option_text: Establish a backup data storage site, preferably at an offsite or separate location, to prevent the loss of critical data and information.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 26-28, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Store backup tapes at either an alternate location or a different room in a fireproof safe. In the event of an incident in the server room, the information on the damaged or destroyed servers would remain intact.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 26-28, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Conduct a feasibility study to duplicate the internal IT system at another location to backup critical data and information in real-time.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 29, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

vulnerability_text: Access to control/computer rooms and remote equipment is not controlled.

options.option_text: Implement access control policies and procedures to secure control/computer rooms and remote equipment. Access control measures to consider include door locks, card readers, intrusion detection sensors, and CCTV.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 23-24, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have a contingency/business continuity plan with the IT service provider for rapid restoration in the event of a service disruption.

options.option_text: Collaborate with the IT service provider to establish a contingency/business continuity plan for restoration.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 26-28, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not participate in a plan with the IT service provider for priority restoration in the event of a service disruption.

options.option_text: Collaborate with the IT service provider to determine if the company prioritizes certain types of facilities for restoration before other customers in the event of a service disruption, and if the facility is eligible for priority restoration.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 26-28, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: If the facility is not eligible for priority restoration before other customers, establish a contingency/business continuity plan to resume facility operations after a service disruption. As part of the planning process, collaborate with the utility provider to review options for rapid restoration of service. Establish a liaison with the utility provider to maintain communications during a service outage. Ensure the contingency/business continuity plan is well understood by facility management and employees.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 24, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have an alternate or backup for IT service to use in case of a loss or disruption of the primary service. The loss of IT could significantly disrupt facility operations.

options.option_text: Evaluate the need for an alternate or backup for IT service in the event that the primary service is lost or disrupted, and explore available options for redundancy.
options.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 29, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility has only one natural gas service connection, creating a potential single point of failure in its natural gas supply.

options.option_text: Collaborate with natural gas utility provider on the feasibility of installing a second service connection that enters the facility in a different location to create redundancy.

vulnerability_text: The natural gas service connections for the facility are not each capable of handling the entire facility load. If service from one connection was disrupted, the natural gas supply for the facility would be degraded.

options.option_text: Collaborate with the natural gas utility provider on the feasibility of modifying service connections such that any one connection could meet the entire facility load.

vulnerability_text: All natural gas service connections for the facility enter at the same geographic location, creating a potential single point of failure in the natural gas supply for the facility.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of separating the natural gas service connections so that they enter the facility at different locations.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The natural gas service connection(s) for the facility is collocated with other utilities (e.g., in utility corridors for electric power, communications, fiber, water): therefore, an incident in one location could disrupt the operations of multiple service systems.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of relocating the natural gas service connection(s) to help mitigate the risk that the catastrophic failure of one utility could inadvertently take down another.

vulnerability_text: There are no protective measures in place for natural gas supply system components inside the facility building(s), leaving them at risk for tampering and accidental or intentional damage.

options.option_text: Secure components of the natural gas system inside of the facility against unauthorized access, for example, with access-controlled utility closets.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), Appendix F, p. F-47, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Evaluate the need to implement enhanced protective measures for natural gas system components, such as intrusion detection sensors and CCTV.
Conduct random security checks of critical interior utility closets and assets.

vulnerability_text: There are no protective measures in place for natural gas supply system components outside the facility building(s), leaving them at risk of accidental or intentional tampering and damage.

options.option_text: Secure components of the natural gas system outside of the facility against unauthorized access.
options.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 2.4.1, p. 2-27, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Evaluate the need to implement enhanced protective measures for natural gas system components, such as intrusion detection sensors and CCTV.
Conduct random security checks of critical exterior natural gas system equipment.

vulnerability_text: The natural gas main and associated equipment is unprotected from accidental or intentional vehicle damage.

options.option_text: Install vehicle barriers to protect the natural gas main and associated equipment from damage.

Explore the feasibility of prohibiting parking near the natural gas main and associated equipment. Utility systems should be located at least 50 feet from loading docks, front entrances, and parking areas.
options.reference: FEMA, 2005, Risk Management Series – Risk Assessment: A How-To Guide to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 452), Appendix A, p. A-11, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1524-20490-7395/fema452_01_05.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have a contingency/business continuity plan with the natural gas service provider for rapid restoration in the event of a service disruption.

options.option_text: Determine the natural gas supply requirements to support facility core operations, and develop commensurate plans in collaboration with the utility provider to ensure service is restored with priority, or alternate service provisions are made. Ensure plans address legitimate hypothetical scenarios that would result in service loss and corresponding restoration timelines so the facility can address business continuity and interim workarounds.
options.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not participate in a plan with the natural gas service utility provider for priority restoration in the event of a service disruption.

options.option_text: Collaborate with the utility provider to determine if the utility prioritizes certain types of facilities for restoration before other customers in the event of a service disruption, and if the facility is eligible for priority restoration.
options.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: If the facility is not eligible for priority restoration before other customers, establish a contingency/business continuity plan to resume facility operations after a service disruption. As part of the planning process, collaborate with the utility provider to review options for rapid restoration of service. Establish a liaison with the utility provider to maintain communications during a service outage. Ensure the contingency/business continuity plan is well understood by facility management and employees.
options.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have an alternate or backup that can be used in the event of a loss of the external natural gas supply. A loss of natural gas could significantly disrupt facility operations.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of establishing an internal natural gas source or an alternative fuel source (e.g., diesel fuel, propane, electricity) to support at least core operations when the external natural gas supply is disrupted.

vulnerability_text: Rail transportation constitutes a single point of failure for facility operations. In the event that this transportation mode was not available, facility operations would be significantly impacted. However, the facility does not participate in a priority plan for restoration following a disruption in service.

options.option_text: Review contracts to determine if the railroad company servicing the facility has MOUs/MOAs with other rail lines to assist if their equipment is not available.
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.7, p. 24.

options.option_text: Explore ways to temporarily decrease the facility's dependency on this mode of transportation, such as alternate work schedules for employees, increased storage of critical materials/products normally transported by rail, etc.
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.7, p. 24.

vulnerability_text: The facility depends on rail transportation for core operations, and no alternative transportation modes would be available if rail service was lost. Facility operations be significantly impacted by the loss of rail transportation.

options.option_text: Include in the facility's business continuity plan immediate, interim, and long-term mitigation measures/strategies for responding to the loss of this critical transportation mode.
options.reference: ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.4.4, p. 18.

options.option_text: Explore options to ship and/or receive critical materials/products normally transported by rail via alternative modes of transportation.
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.7, p. 24.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of increasing onsite storage of critical materials/products normally received by rail to mitigate the impacts of a disruption in rail service.

vulnerability_text: Air transportation constitutes a single point of failure for facility operations. In the event that this transportation mode was not available, facility operations would be significantly impacted. However, the facility does not participate in a priority plan for restoration following a disruption in service.

options.option_text: Review contracts to determine if the air carrier servicing the facility has MOUs/MOAs with other carriers to assist if their equipment is not available.
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.7, p. 24.

options.option_text: Explore ways to temporarily decrease the facility's dependency on this mode of transportation, such as alternate work schedules for employees, increased storage of critical materials/products normally transported by air, etc.
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.7, p. 24.

vulnerability_text: The facility depends on air transportation for core operations, and no alternative transportation modes would be available if air service was lost. Facility operations would be significantly impacted by the loss of air transportation.

options.option_text: Include in the facility's business continuity plan immediate, interim, and long-term mitigation measures/strategies for responding to the loss of this critical transportation mode.
options.reference: ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.4.4, p. 18.

options.option_text: Explore options to ship and/or receive materials/products normally transported by air via alternative modes of transportation.
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.7, p. 24.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of increasing onsite storage of materials/products normally received by air to mitigate the impacts of a disruption in air service.

vulnerability_text: Road transportation constitutes a single point of failure for facility operations. In the event of severe damage to or destruction of road infrastructure, facility operations would be significantly impacted. However, the facility does not participate in a priority plan for restoration of this transportation mode.

options.option_text: Collaborate with the appropriate agencies (e.g., State and/or local transportation department, State and/or local EMA) to share information about the facility's critical dependence on road infrastructure. Explore the feasibility of entering into agreements about priority restoration of this transportation mode (e.g., plowing roads, clearing debris, repairing or replacing damaged road infrastructure) in the event of a disruption.
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.7, p. 24.

options.option_text: Explore ways to temporarily decrease the facility's dependency on this mode of transportation, such as alternate work schedules for employees, increased storage of critical materials/products normally shipped/received by the single road to the facility, etc.
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.7, p. 24.

vulnerability_text: The facility depends on road transportation for core operations, and no alternative transportation modes would be available if the single road to the facility was severely damaged or destroyed. Facility operations would be significantly impacted by the loss of this road.

options.option_text: Include in the facility's business continuity plan immediate, interim, and long-term mitigation measures/strategies for responding to the loss of this critical transportation mode.
options.reference: ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.4.4, p. 18.

options.option_text: Explore options to ship and/or receive materials/products normally transported by this road via alternative modes of transportation.
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.7, p. 24.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of increasing onsite storage of materials/products normally received by this road to mitigate the impacts of a disruption,
Evaluate the need to maintain heavy equipment onsite to clear the road of obstructions when necessary.

vulnerability_text: Maritime transportation constitutes a single point of failure for facility operations. In the event that this transportation mode was not available, facility operations would be significantly impacted. However, the facility does not participate in a priority plan for restoration following a disruption in service.

options.option_text: Review contracts to determine if the maritime transportation provider servicing the facility has MOUs/MOAs with other providers to assist if their equipment is not available.
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.7, p. 24.

options.option_text: Explore ways to temporarily decrease the facility's dependency on this mode of transportation, such as alternate work schedules for employees, increased storage of critical materials/products normally transported on the water, etc.
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.7, p. 24.

vulnerability_text: The facility depends on maritime transportation for core operations, and no alternative transportation modes exist. Facility operations would be significantly impacted by the loss of maritime transportation.

options.option_text: Include in the facility's business continuity plan immediate, interim, and long-term mitigation measures/strategies for responding to the loss of this critical transportation mode.
options.reference: ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.4.4, p. 18.

options.option_text: Explore options to ship and/or receive materials/products normally transported on the water via alternative modes of transportation.
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.7, p. 24.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of increasing onsite storage of materials/products normally received by maritime transportation to mitigate the impacts of a disruption.

vulnerability_text: The facility depends on pipeline transportation for the receipt of critical materials/products. In the event of severe damage to or destruction of the pipeline(s), facility operations could be significantly impacted. However, the facility does not participate in a priority plan for pipeline restoration after a loss of service.

options.option_text: Collaborate with the pipeline company to share information about the facility's critical dependence on this infrastructure. Explore the feasibility of entering into agreements about priority restoration of this pipeline in the event of a disruption.
Explore the feasibility of increasing onsite storage of critical materials/products normally received by pipeline to mitigate the impacts of a disruption in service.

vulnerability_text: The facility depends on pipeline transportation for the shipment of products. In the event of severe damage to or destruction of the pipeline(s), facility operations could be significantly impacted. However, the facility does not participate in a priority plan for pipeline restoration after a loss of service.

options.option_text: Collaborate with the pipeline company to share information about the facility's critical dependence on this infrastructure. Explore the feasibility of entering into agreements about priority restoration of this pipeline in the event of a disruption.
Explore the feasibility of increasing onsite storage of critical materials/products normally shipped by pipeline to mitigate the impacts of a disruption in service.

vulnerability_text: The facility depends on pipeline transport for the receipt of critical products, and no alternative modes would be available if pipeline transport was lost. Facility operations would be significantly impacted by the loss of pipeline transport.

options.option_text: Include in the facility's business continuity plan immediate, interim, and long-term mitigation measures/strategies for responding to the loss of this critical transportation mode.
options.reference: ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.4.4, p. 18.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of increasing onsite storage of critical materials/products normally received by pipeline to mitigate the impacts of a disruption in service.

vulnerability_text: The facility depends on pipeline transport for the shipment of products, and no alternative modes would be available if pipeline transport was lost. Facility operations would be significantly impacted by the loss of pipeline transport.

options.option_text: Include in the facility's business continuity plan immediate, interim, and long-term mitigation measures/strategies for responding to the loss of this critical transportation mode.
options.reference: ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.4.4, p. 18.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of increasing onsite storage of critical materials/products normally shipped by pipeline to mitigate the impacts of a disruption in service.

vulnerability_text: The facility has only one service connection for the removal of wastewater from the site, creating a potential single point of failure. Disruption of service from this single pipeline could degrade facility operations.

options.option_text: Collaborate with the wastewater utility provider on the feasibility of installing a second service connection that enters the facility in a different location to create redundancy.

vulnerability_text: The wastewater service connections for the facility are not each capable of handling the entire facility load. If service from one connection was disrupted, wastewater removal service for the facility would be degraded.

options.option_text: Collaborate with the wastewater utility provider on the feasibility of implementing infrastructure upgrades to mitigate potential impacts from the disruption of one of the wastewater service connections to the facility.

vulnerability_text: All wastewater service connections for the facility enter at the same geographic location, creating a potential single point of failure in the wastewater removal service supply for the facility.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of separating the wastewater service connections so they enter the facility at different locations.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87 to 3-91, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The wastewater service connection(s) for the facility is collocated with other utilities (e.g., in utility corridors for electric power, communications, fiber, natural gas): therefore, an incident in one location could disrupt the operations of multiple service systems.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of relocating the wastewater service connection(s) to help mitigate the risk that the catastrophic failure of one utility could inadvertently take down another.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87 to 3-91, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: There are no protective measures in place for wastewater service system components inside the facility building(s), leaving them at risk for tampering and accidental or intentional damage.

options.option_text: Secure components of the wastewater system inside the facility against unauthorized access, for example, with access-controlled utility closets.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87 to 3-91, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Evaluate the need to implement enhanced protective measures for wastewater system components, such as intrusion detection sensors and CCTV.
Conduct random security checks of critical interior utility closets and assets.

vulnerability_text: There are no protective measures in place for wastewater service system components outside the facility building(s), leaving them at risk for tampering and accidental or intentional damage.

options.option_text: Secure components of the wastewater system outside of the facility against unauthorized access.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87 to 3-91, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Secure manhole covers and access points.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.12, p. 5-33, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: Evaluate the need to implement enhanced protective measures for wastewater system components, such as intrusion detection sensors and CCTV.
Conduct random security checks of critical exterior wastewater system assets.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have a contingency/business continuity plan with the wastewater service provider for rapid restoration in the event of a service disruption.

options.option_text: Determine the wastewater service requirements to support facility core operations, and develop commensurate plans in collaboration with the utility provider to ensure service is restored with priority, or alternate service provisions are made. Ensure plans address legitimate hypothetical scenarios that would result in service loss and corresponding restoration timelines so the facility can address business continuity and interim workarounds.
options.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not participate in a plan with the wastewater service utility provider for priority restoration in the event of a service disruption.

options.option_text: Collaborate with the utility provider to determine if the utility prioritizes certain types of facilities for restoration before other customers in the event of a service disruption, and if the facility is eligible for priority restoration.
options.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: If the facility is not eligible for priority restoration before other customers, establish a contingency/business continuity plan to resume facility operations after a service disruption. As part of the planning process, collaborate with the utility provider to review options for rapid restoration of service. Establish a liaison with the utility provider to maintain communications during a service outage. Ensure the contingency/business continuity plan is well understood by facility management and employees.
options.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have an alternate to the external wastewater discharge service. A disruption to or loss of wastewater discharge service could significantly disrupt facility operations.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of establishing an alternate wastewater discharge service (e.g., portable toilets) to use if the external wastewater discharge service is disrupted.

vulnerability_text: The facility has only one water service connection, creating a potential single point of failure. Disruption of service from this single pipeline could degrade facility operations.

options.option_text: Collaborate with water utility service provider on the feasibility of installing a second service connection that enters the facility in a different location to create redundancy.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87 to 3-91, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The water service connections for the facility are not each capable of handling the entire facility load. If service from one connection was disrupted, the water supply to the facility would be degraded.

options.option_text: Consult with the water utility provider and inquire about the feasibility of installing an additional connection that is capable of handling the entire facility load.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87 to 3-91, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: All water service connections for the facility enter at the same geographic location, creating a potential single point of failure in the water supply for the facility.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of separating the water service connections so that they enter the facility at different locations.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87 to 3-91, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The water service connection(s) for the facility is collocated with other utilities (e.g., in utility corridors for electric power, communications, fiber, natural gas): therefore, an incident in one location could disrupt the operations of multiple service systems.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of relocating the water service connection(s) to help mitigate the risk that the catastrophic failure of one utility could inadvertently take down another.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87 to 3-91, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: There are no protective measures in place for water service system components inside the facility building(s), leaving them at risk for tampering and accidental or intentional damage.

options.option_text: Secure components of the water system inside the facility against unauthorized access, for example, with access-controlled utility closets.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87 to 3-91, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Evaluate the need to implement enhanced protective measures for water system components, such as intrusion detection sensors and CCTV.
Conduct random security checks of critical interior utility closets and assets.

vulnerability_text: There are no protective measures in place for water supply system components outside the facility building(s), leaving them at risk of accidental or intentional tampering and damage.

options.option_text: Secure components of the water system outside of the facility against unauthorized access.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87 to 3-91, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Determine if access to water supply system components is required for maintenance. If so, secure openings with screening, grating, latticework, or other similar devices so that access points do not allow unauthorized access.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.12, p. 5-33, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: Secure manhole covers 10 inches or more in diameter, for example, with locks and hasps, by welding them shut, or by appropriate bolting to their frames. Ensure that hasps, locks, and bolts are made of materials that resist corrosion. Keyed bolts (which make removal by unauthorized personnel more difficult) are also available.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.12, p. 5-33, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: Evaluate the need to implement enhanced protective measures for water system components, such as intrusion detection sensors and CCTV.
Conduct random security checks of critical exterior water system assets.

vulnerability_text: The water supply is unprotected from accidental or intentional vehicle damage.

options.option_text: Install vehicle barriers to protect the water supply from damage.
Explore the feasibility of prohibiting parking near the water supply. Utility systems should be located at least 50 feet from loading docks, front entrances, and parking areas.
options.reference: FEMA, 2005, Risk Management Series – Risk Assessment: A How-To Guide to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 452), Appendix A, p. A-11, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1524-20490-7395/fema452_01_05.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have a contingency/business continuity plan with the water service provider for rapid restoration in the event of a service disruption.

options.option_text: Determine the water supply requirements to support facility core operations, and develop commensurate plans in collaboration with the utility provider to ensure service is restored with priority, or alternate service provisions are made. Ensure plans address legitimate hypothetical scenarios that would result in service loss and corresponding restoration timelines so the facility can address business continuity and interim workarounds.
options.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not participate in a plan with the water service utility provider for priority restoration in the event of a service disruption.

options.option_text: Collaborate with the utility provider to determine if the utility prioritizes certain types of facilities for restoration before other customers in the event of a service disruption, and if the facility is eligible for priority restoration.
options.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: If the facility is not eligible for priority restoration before other customers, establish a contingency/business continuity plan to resume facility operations after a service disruption. As part of the planning process, collaborate with the utility provider to review options for rapid restoration of service. Establish a liaison with the utility provider to maintain communications during a service outage. Ensure the contingency/business continuity plan is well understood by facility management and employees.
options.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have an alternate source of water to use as a backup in the event of a disruption to or loss of the primary water supply.

options.option_text: Collaborate with the water utility service provider to evaluate alternative water supply options. It is critical to engage with the water utility service provider when planning and implementing such contingencies.

Conduct a water audit to understand the facility's water usage, then evaluate alternative water supply options. It is critical to engage with the water utility service provider throughout the duration of this process.
options.reference: CDC and American Water Works Association, 2012, Emergency Water Supply Planning Guide for Hospitals and Health Care Facilities, http://www.cdc.gov/healthywater/pdf/emergency/emergency-water-supply-planning-guide.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: Conduct a water audit to understand the facility's water usage, then evaluate alternative water supply options. Based on analysis of the water use audit and the availability of alternative water supply options, develop a written emergency water supply plan for the facility. Complete activities necessary to ensure the availability of alternate emergency water supplies. Practice and exercise the emergency water supply plan. It is critical to engage with the water utility service provider throughout the duration of this process.
options.reference: CDC and American Water Works Association, 2012, Emergency Water Supply Planning Guide for Hospitals and Health Care Facilities, http://www.cdc.gov/healthywater/pdf/emergency/emergency-water-supply-planning-guide.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility lacks a backup water supply for the fire suppression system. Loss of fire suppression capabilities could cause the facility to shut down until water service is restored.

options.option_text: Collaborate with the water utility service provider to evaluate alternative water supply options for the fire suppression system. It is critical to engage with the water utility service provider when planning and implementing such contingencies.

vulnerability_text: The facility has an alternative source of water to use in the event of a disruption to or loss of the primary water supply, but the alternate cannot support full core operations.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of upgrading the alternative source currently in place, or installing another alternative source to augment the current system, to meet water requirements for core operations during an extended outage of primary service.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have an exterior IDS. An exterior IDS can provide the facility with increased capabilities for detection of attempted and successful exterior security breaches, allowing for more rapid response to intrusions and thereby preventing further damage to the facility.

options.option_text: Evaluate the weakest areas of the exterior and install IDS sensors as appropriate. Types of sensors used to detect exterior intrusion include buried-line sensors, fence-associated sensors, and freestanding sensors.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 97-113.

options.option_text:
Evaluate the weakest areas of the exterior and install IDS sensors as appropriate. The basic
types of sensors used for exterior intrusion are buried-line sensors, fence-associated sensors, and freestanding sensors. The IDS should be evenly distributed throughout critical areas to ensure uniform detection around the entire length of the facility’s perimeter.
