options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.4.3, p. 2-69, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The backup power supply covers emergency lighting for only critical locations along fences and/or gates and/or in parking areas. The backup power supply does not cover most of the existing lights.

options.option_text: Ensure that the backup power supply covers most existing lights, not only lights for critical locations.

vulnerability_text: Although the backup power supply covers emergency lighting for fences, gates, and/or parking areas, it does not cover emergency lighting for most critical locations in these areas.

options.option_text: Ensure that the backup power supply covers emergency lighting for most, if not all, critical locations.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have portable lighting capabilities onsite to provide lighting for fences, gates, and/or parking areas during emergencies or elevated threat situations.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of procuring portable lighting capabilities to provide coverage for these areas, as needed.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.4.3, p. 2-69, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

vulnerability_text: Building entrances and delivery areas are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.

options.option_text: Install lighting for building entrances and delivery areas. Before installation, determine the appropriate type of lighting based on the overall requirements of the site and the building (for example, continuous or standby). In addition, consider operational costs, such as life-cycle costs for energy and maintenance because of their effect on project sustainability.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.4.3, p. 2-67 to 2-69, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014; Garcia, M.L., 2006, Vulnerability Assessment of Physical Protection Systems, Burlington, MA: Elsevier, p. 136-138.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of implementing the following:
Installing two or more lighting units at pedestrian entrances, that provide adequate illumination for recognizing individuals and examining credentials.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 5-12, August, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 13, 2014.

options.option_text: Installing two lighting units at vehicle access points, to facilitate the complete inspection of passenger cars, trucks, etc., as well as their contents and passengers.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 5-12, August, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 13, 2014.

options.option_text: Ensure that lighting is compatible with CCTV systems, as the cameras may need high intensity, low intensity, or infrared light for proper operation.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.4.3, p. 2-68, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Consult Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, for more information about security lighting.

vulnerability_text: Lighting for building entrances and delivery areas does not overlap. Lighting that does not overlap can cause shadows or dark areas where intruders can go undetected.

options.option_text: Update the lighting system to include overlapping cones of light to eliminate or reduce shadows and dark areas.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.4.3, p. 2-68, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Update the lighting system to ensure illumination uniformity.

options.option_text: Update the lighting system to ensure illumination uniformity, so that security personnel can see ahead and to the sides with an absence of dark areas caused by shadows. Lighting should be brightest in secure areas, with the light gradually less in areas adjacent to high-illumination areas.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 5-5, August, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 13, 2014.

options.option_text: Establish a maintenance protocol to ensure that system components are regularly inspected and repaired or replaced as necessary. Ensure that lighting fixtures are clean and properly aimed.

options.option_text: Expand the coverage of the backup power supply to include emergency lighting for these areas, so that in the event of a power failure or other emergency, illumination is available.

options.option_text: Ensure that the backup power supply covers most existing lights, not only lights for critical locations.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have portable lighting capabilities onsite to provide lighting for building entrances and delivery areas during emergencies or elevated threat situations.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of procuring portable lighting capabilities to provide coverage for these areas, as needed.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.4.3, p. 2-69, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

vulnerability_text: Waterside facilities are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.

options.option_text: Install lighting for waterside facilities. Before installation, determine the appropriate type of lighting based on the overall requirements of the site and the building (for example, continuous or standby). In addition, consider operational costs, such as life-cycle costs for energy and maintenance, when designing an appropriate lighting situation, because of their effect on project sustainability.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.4.3, p. 2-67 to 2-69, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014; Garcia, M.L., 2006, Vulnerability Assessment of Physical Protection Systems, Burlington, MA: Elsevier, p. 136-138.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of implementing the following:
Illuminating water approaches and the pier area. The lighting must not in any way violate marine rules and regulations (for example, it must not be glaring to pilots).
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 5-13, August, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 13, 2014.

options.option_text: Illuminating decks on open piers to at least 1 foot-candle, and the water approaches (extending to a distance of 100 feet from the pier) to at least 0.5 foot-candles. (A foot-candle is a unit of illuminance on a surface that is everywhere 1 foot from a uniform point source of light of one candle and equal to 1 lumen per square foot. To convert foot-candles to watts, first convert foot-candles into lumens, then convert lumens into watts. One foot-candle equals 10.76 lumens, and 1 lumen equals 0.001496 watts.)
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 5-13, August, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 13, 2014; Merriam Webster, 2014, "Foot-candle," Dictionary, http://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/foot-candle, accessed June 13, 2014.

options.option_text: Illuminating the area beneath the pier floor with small-wattage floodlights arranged on the piling.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 5-13, August, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 13, 2014.

options.option_text: Providing portable lighting as part of the protective lighting system for piers and docks.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 5-13, August, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 13, 2014.

options.option_text: Consult the U.S. Coast Guard for approval of protective lighting adjacent to navigable waters.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 5-13, August, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 13, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not exchange information with the FBI. Information exchange with the FBI would enhance the security and resilience posture of the facility.

options.option_text: Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the FBI. The FBI has a variety of major information sharing initiatives for sharing timely, relevant, and actionable intelligence. For more information, visit the FBI Website at http://www.fbi.gov/stats-services/publications/national-information-sharing-strategy-1.

Consult your local police department, sheriff's office, and/or PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the JTTF. Information about JTTFs is also available via the FBI Website, at http://www.fbi.gov/about-us/investigate/terrorism/terrorism_jttfs.
Establish a liaison with the FBI to enhance communications, coordination, and cooperation.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not exchange information with Federal law enforcement agencies such as FPS, TSA, and ICE. Information exchange with Federal law enforcement agencies would enhance the security and resilience posture of the facility.

options.option_text: Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the appropriate Federal law enforcement agencies.
Consult your local police department, sheriff's office, and/or PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the appropriate Federal law enforcement agencies.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not exchange information with the JTTF (or equivalent). Information exchange with the JTTF would enhance the security and resilience posture of the facility.

options.option_text: Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the appropriate ISAC. Additional information is available via the National Council of ISACs Website, at http://www.isaccouncil.org.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not exchange information through the HSIN portal. Using the HSIN portal for information sharing would enhance the security and resilience posture of the facility.

options.option_text: Consult your local PSA to become a member of the HSIN community. Information about HSIN is also available via the DHS Website, at http://www.dhs.gov/homeland-security-information-network.
Consult your local PSA to better understand how HSIN's features and capabilities can enhance information sharing with your mission partners.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not exchange information via InfraGard. Becoming a member of InfraGard would enhance the security and resilience posture of the facility.

options.option_text: Consult your local PSA about becoming a member of InfraGard. Additional information is also available via the InfraGard Website, at https://www.infragard.org.

Consult your local police department, sheriff's office, and/or PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the U.S. Attorney's Office ATAC.
Contact your district U.S. Attorney's Office regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the ATAC.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not exchange information with DHS. Information exchange with DHS would enhance the security and resilience posture of the facility.

options.option_text: Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with DHS.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not exchange information with NOAA. Information exchange with NOAA would enhance the resilience posture of the facility.

options.option_text: Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with NOAA. Guidance on the use of and access to National Weather Service watches, warnings, advisories, and other similar products is available via the NOAA Website, at http://alerts.weather.gov.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not exchange information with USGS. Information exchange with USGS would enhance the resilience posture of the facility.

options.option_text: Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with USGS. Information about USGS data catalogs and portals is available via the USGS Website, at http://www.usgs.gov/datamanagement/share/usgscatalogs.php.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not exchange information with the CDC. Information exchange with the CDC would enhance the resilience posture of the facility.

options.option_text: Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with the CDC.

The CDC’s PHIN is a national initiative to increase the capacity of public health agencies to electronically exchange data and information across organizations and jurisdictions (e.g., clinical care to public health, public health to public health and public health to other federal agencies). Contact PHIN by phone or email, at 770-454-4863 or PHINTECH@CDC.GOV, or visit the CDC Website for more information, at http://www.cdc.gov/phin/about/index.html.

The CDC Office for State, Tribal, Local and Territorial Support includes the CoPs Program: CoPs provide a collaborative framework for public health professionals to share information and expertise. To join a CoP, sign up at http://www.phconnect.org or visit the Join a CoP Webpage, at http://www.cdc.gov/phcommunities/join.html.

options.option_text: Consult your local PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with State/local agencies, such as State and/or major urban area fusion centers, State CIP Coordinator, State HSA, State/local EMA, State/local law enforcement, and State/local public health agencies.

Consult your local police department, sheriff's office, and/or PSA regarding opportunities to enhance information sharing with State/local agencies, such as State and/or major urban area fusion centers, State CIP Coordinator, State HSA, State/local EMA, State/local law enforcement, and State/local public health agencies.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have written MOUs/MOAs with other entities other than emergency responders (e.g., neighboring facilities, cleanup contractors, water and wastewater agency response networks). Such agreements would facilitate critical assistance in time of need.

options.option_text: Assess the need to establish prearrangements to augment facility resources during elevated threat situations, emergencies, and other circumstances. Collaborate with the appropriate agencies to establish written agreements for assistance.
options.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.4.7, p. 35, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 2, 2014.

vulnerability_text: None of the facility's employees have U.S. government security clearance. Without clearances, facility personnel cannot review classified material related to pertinent threats.

options.option_text: Consult your local PSA for more information about the DHS Private Sector Clearance Program, which sponsors security clearances for certain private-sector officials, including critical infrastructure owners/operators, sector leadership, and subject matter experts.

vulnerability_text: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate hurricane impacts.

options.option_text: Install permanent storm shutters to protect windows to help prevent glass breakage and stop windborne objects from penetrating the facility. Always use permanent storm shutters in areas where there will be little advance warning of high winds, as permanent shutters can be closed quickly, unlike temporary plywood covers that must be retrieved from storage and mounted with bolts or screws. Consult the FEMA flyer Protect Windows and Doors with Covers, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-8875/how2017_how2029_protect_windows_doors_4_11.pdf, for more information.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Protect Windows and Doors with Covers, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-8875/how2017_how2029_protect_windows_doors_4_11.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Explore options to retrofit roofing to secure it to the structure and increase resistance to high winds. Consult the FEMA Webpage "Protect Your Property from High Winds," at http://www.fema.gov/media-library/assets/documents/13270?id=3263, for more information.
Securely anchor rooftop equipment (e.g., HVAC units, lightning protection systems, antennae) to prevent them from detaching during high winds.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Design Guide for Improving Critical Facility Safety from Flooding and High Winds (FEMA 543), section 3.3.4, p. 3-81 to 3-97, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1557-20490-1542/fema543_complete.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Conduct a vulnerability assessment for electrical and mechanical utilities and systems to understand the facility's susceptibility to damage due to hurricane-related flooding.
options.reference: FEMA, 2013, Floodproofing Non-Residential Buildings (FEMA P-936), section 2.6.3, p. 2-48 to 2-51, July, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/9a50c534fc5895799321dcdd4b6083e7/P-936_8-20-13_508r.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Explore flood-proofing retrofits such as dry floodproofing (i.e., measures resulting in watertight, flood-resistant structures), wet flood-proofing (i.e., the use of flood-damage-resistant materials and construction techniques to minimize flood damage), floodwalls, and levees. Consult Floodproofing Non-Residential Buildings (FEMA P-936), available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/9a50c534fc5895799321dcdd4b6083e7/P-936_8-20-13_508r.pdf, for more information.
Relocate utility installations and other critical assets away from rooms and locations that are below-grade and susceptible to flooding-related damage.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Design Guide for Improving Critical Facility Safety from Flooding and High Winds (FEMA 543), section 2.4.6, p. 2-85, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1557-20490-1542/fema543_complete.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Protect the facility's backup power generator and its fuel tank, as well as electrical and fuel supply lines, from destructive natural hazards. In flood hazard areas, mount the generator and tank securely on concrete platforms, above the expected flood level. If possible, move the generator and tank inside or next to a building or protective structure to shield them from severe weather events such as wind. Consult the FEMA flyer Install a Generator for Emergency Power, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf, for more information.

Explore the feasibility of procuring a backup power generator to power life safety systems in the event that a natural hazard disrupts power to the facility and its critical assets. Consult the FEMA flyer Install a Generator for Emergency Power, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf, for more information.
Install the necessary wiring and transfer switches to connect to a backup power generator and plan for its rapid deployment.

Explore the feasibility of using natural gas to fuel the backup power system. While diesel, which is traditionally used to fuel backup power systems, is relatively safe to store onsite and economical to procure, re-supply for an extended period may be an issue. Because most natural gas infrastructure is underground, the natural gas supply may not be impacted by severe weather events.
options.reference: Hamilton, R., 2007, Power topic #7006 -Lessons in emergency power preparedness: Planning in the wake of Katrina, Cummins Power Generation Inc., p.3, November, http://www.cumminspower.com/www/literature/technicalpapers/PT-7006-Standby-Katrina-en.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014; ICF International, 2013, Combined Heat and Power: Enabling Resilient Energy Infrastructure for Critical Facilities, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, p. 6, March, http://www1.eere.energy.gov/manufacturing/distributedenergy/pdfs/chp_critical_facilities.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/hurricanes, for more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures during hurricane season.

options.option_text: Establish specific plans and procedures for the duration of hurricane season. Contact the State/local emergency management office and/or the regional FEMA office for available hurricane planning resources.

Develop shelter-in-place plans for facility occupants if it is not safe to evacuate the facility during a natural disaster. Train employees on the plan, and conduct recurring exercises on, reviews of, and updates to the plan. Ensure contractors and frequent visitors to the facility are aware of these plans. Consult the Ready.gov Website, available at http://www.ready.gov/shelter, for more information on sheltering.
Establish a hurricane planning checklist that can be provided to all facility occupants ahead of hurricane season.
options.reference: Nadel, B.A., 2004, Building Security: Handbook for Architectural Planning and Design, New York, NY: McGraw-Hill, ch. 12, p. 12.

options.option_text: Develop a plan for dealing with power outages and loss of elevator service in high-rise buildings. Ensure the plan includes the location of alternate exits for occupants.
options.reference: Nadel, B.A., 2004, Building Security: Handbook for Architectural Planning and Design, New York, NY: McGraw-Hill, ch. 12, p. 12.

options.option_text: Ensure all trees on facility property are far enough away to prevent them from damaging the facility if they should fall. Consult the FEMA flyer Remove Trees and Potential Windborne Missiles, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-3107/how2020_trees_missiles_4_11.pdf, for more information.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Remove Trees and Potential Windborne Missiles, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-3107/how2020_trees_missiles_4_11.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Keep limbs on nearby trees trimmed to prevent breakage. Falling tree limbs can damage facilities, assets, and critical utility lines.

If the facility does not have a backup generator, explore options to enter into a contract to have one readily available, in the event that a natural hazard disrupts power to the facility and its critical assets. Ensure that the facility is equipped (i.e., has the necessary wiring and transfer switches) to facilitate the rapid installation and operation of the generator unit.
Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/hurricanes, for more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have deployable (i.e., non-permanent) mitigation measures to be put in place in anticipation of a hurricane.

options.option_text: If the facility does not have permanent storm shutters, establish plans to board windows with exterior-grade or marine plywood. Ensure temporary covers are readily accessible, and use a numbering or lettering system that shows which cover goes with which window.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Protect Windows and Doors with Covers, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-8875/how2017_how2029_protect_windows_doors_4_11.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Establish plans to deploy emergency flood mitigation measures, such as sandbags, temporary flood barriers, and flood wrapping systems. Consult Floodproofing Non-Residential Buildings (FEMA P-936), available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/9a50c534fc5895799321dcdd4b6083e7/P-936_8-20-13_508r.pdf, for more information.
Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/hurricanes, for more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate flood impacts.

options.option_text: Conduct a vulnerability assessment for electrical and mechanical utilities and systems to understand the facility's susceptibility to damage due to severe flooding.
options.reference: FEMA, 2013, Floodproofing Non-Residential Buildings (FEMA P-936), section 2.6.3, p. 2-48 to 2-51, July, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/9a50c534fc5895799321dcdd4b6083e7/P-936_8-20-13_508r.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Explore flood-proofing retrofits such as dry floodproofing (i.e., measures resulting in watertight, flood-resistant structures), wet flood-proofing (i.e., the use of flood-damage-resistant materials and construction techniques to minimize flood damage), floodwalls, and levees. Consult Floodproofing Non-Residential Buildings (FEMA P-936), available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/9a50c534fc5895799321dcdd4b6083e7/P-936_8-20-13_508r.pdf, for more information.
Relocate utility installations and other critical assets away from rooms and locations that are below-grade and susceptible to flooding-related damage.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Design Guide for Improving Critical Facility Safety from Flooding and High Winds (FEMA 543), section 2.4.6, p. 2-85, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1557-20490-1542/fema543_complete.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Protect the facility's backup power generator and its fuel tank, as well as electrical and fuel supply lines, from destructive natural hazards. In flood hazard areas, mount the generator and tank securely on concrete platforms, above the expected flood level. Consult the FEMA flyer Install a Generator for Emergency Power, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf, for more information.

Explore the feasibility of procuring a backup power generator to power life safety systems in the event that a natural hazard disrupts power to the facility and its critical assets. Consult the FEMA flyer Install a Generator for Emergency Power, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf, for more information.
Install the necessary wiring and transfer switches to connect to a backup power generator and plan for its rapid deployment.

Explore the feasibility of using natural gas to fuel the backup power system. While diesel, which is traditionally used to fuel backup power systems, is relatively safe to store onsite and economical to procure, re-supply for an extended period may be an issue. Because most natural gas infrastructure is underground, the natural gas supply may not be impacted by severe weather events.
options.reference: Hamilton, R., 2007, Power topic #7006 -Lessons in emergency power preparedness: Planning in the wake of Katrina, Cummins Power Generation Inc., p.3, November, http://www.cumminspower.com/www/literature/technicalpapers/PT-7006-Standby-Katrina-en.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014; ICF International, 2013, Combined Heat and Power: Enabling Resilient Energy Infrastructure for Critical Facilities, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, p. 6, March, http://www1.eere.energy.gov/manufacturing/distributedenergy/pdfs/chp_critical_facilities.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/floods, for more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures for flooding.

options.option_text: Establish specific plans and procedures to protect the facility and its critical assets from flooding damage. Contact the State/local emergency management office and/or the regional FEMA office for available flood planning resources.
Establish a flood response checklist that can be provided to employees ahead of a flood watch and/or warning.
options.reference: Nadel, B.A., 2004, Building Security: Handbook for Architectural Planning and Design, New York, NY: McGraw-Hill, ch. 12, p. 9.

options.option_text: Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/floods, for more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have deployable (i.e., non-permanent) mitigation measures to be put in place in anticipation of flooding.

options.option_text: Establish plans to deploy emergency flood mitigation measures, such as sandbags, temporary flood barriers, and flood wrapping systems. Consult Floodproofing Non-Residential Buildings (FEMA P-936), available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/9a50c534fc5895799321dcdd4b6083e7/P-936_8-20-13_508r.pdf, for more information.
Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/floods, for more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate earthquake impacts.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of modifying or retrofitting the facility to make it safer during earthquakes and more resistant to earthquake damage and disruption. The optimal combination earthquake risk-reduction measures for a facility will depend on factors such as the potential severity of the earthquake hazards in the area, the current condition of the facility, whether the facility is owned or leased, and how vulnerable facility operations are to facility damage and associated downtime. Consult QuakeSmart, at http://www.fema.gov/quakesmart, for information about how to assess facility risks and how to develop and implement a plan to cost-effectively mitigate those risks.
options.reference: FEMA, 2013, "Earthquake Safety at Work," January 18, http://www.fema.gov/earthquake/earthquake-safety-work, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: If the facility has cripple walls, brace the walls by adding 2-inch by 4-inch boards between vertical studs at the top and bottom of the walls and, if necessary, at other locations between the studs. Consult the FEMA flyer Brace Cripple Walls, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0271/how2012_brace_cripple_walls.pdf, for more information.
options.reference: FEMA, 2008, Brace Cripple Walls, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0271/how2012_brace_cripple_walls.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Anchor any sill plates. Sill plates rest directly on top of a foundation, and bolting the plates directly into the foundation provides extra stability. Consult the FEMA flyer Bolt Sill Plates to Foundation, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-5339/how2013_sill_plates.pdf, for more information.
options.reference: FEMA, 2008, Bolt Sill Plates to Foundation, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-5339/how2013_sill_plates.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of retrofitting existing gas or water lines with flexible connections to prevent damage. Consult the FEMA flyer Use Flexible Connection on Gas and Water Lines, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-7131/how2014_flex_connections.pdf, for more information.
options.reference: FEMA, 2008, Use Flexible Connection on Gas and Water Lines, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-7131/how2014_flex_connections.pdf.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of anchoring heavy equipment directly to the floor or another suitable part of the building. If this is not possible, mount equipment on vibration isolators. Consult the FEMA flyer Anchor Equipment Properly, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-9548/how2023_anchor_equip.pdf, for more information.
options.reference: FEMA, 2008, Anchor Equipment Properly, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-9548/how2023_anchor_equip.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of procuring a backup power generator to power life safety systems in the event that a natural hazard disrupts power to the facility and its critical assets. Consult the FEMA flyer Install a Generator for Emergency Power, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf, for more information.
Install the necessary wiring and transfer switches to connect to a backup power generator and plan for its rapid deployment.
Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/earthquakes, for more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures for earthquakes.

options.option_text: Establish earthquake emergency plans and procedures. Emphasize safe places for building occupants to "drop, cover, and hold on" during earthquake shaking. Establish safe locations where people can rendezvous when the shaking has stopped and it is safe and advisable to evacuate the facility. Contact the State/local emergency management office and/or the regional FEMA office for available earthquake planning resources.
options.reference: FEMA, 2013, "Earthquake Safety at Work," January 18, http://www.fema.gov/earthquake/earthquake-safety-work, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Conduct periodic, mandatory earthquake drills for employees to practice earthquake response procedures and condition themselves to react spontaneously and safely.
options.reference: FEMA, 2013, "Earthquake Safety at Work," January 18, http://www.fema.gov/earthquake/earthquake-safety-work, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Arrange for employees to be trained in first aid, CPR, and fire extinguisher use to help protect facility occupants in the immediate aftermath of an earthquake.
options.reference: FEMA, 2013, "Earthquake Safety at Work," January 18, http://www.fema.gov/earthquake/earthquake-safety-work, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Explore opportunities to engage in Great ShakeOut Earthquake Drills, which are annual events for communities to collectively practice and improve earthquake preparedness. Consult the Great ShakeOut Earthquake Drills Website, at http://www.shakeout.org, for more information.
Anchor overhead lighting fixtures, brace top-heavy objects, fasten all wall objects securely, and place all large or heavy objects in low positions (e.g., on the floor, lower shelves).
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Fact Sheet: Earthquakes, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1621-20490-0014/earthquakesfactsheet_final.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: If the facility does not have a backup generator, explore options to enter into a contract to have one readily available, in the event that a natural hazard disrupts power to the facility and its critical assets. Ensure that the facility is equipped (i.e., has the necessary wiring and transfer switches) to facilitate the rapid installation and operation of the generator unit.
Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/earthquakes, for more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate tornado impacts.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of establishing a safe room or rooms specifically designed to meet FEMA criteria and provide "near-absolute protection" during tornadoes. To be considered a FEMA safe room, the structure must be designed and constructed to the guidelines specified in FEMA P-320, Taking Shelter from the Storm: Building a Safe Room for Your Home or Small Business (available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1454-20490-8914/fema_p_320.pdf), and FEMA P-361, Design and Construction Guidance for Community Safe Rooms (available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1508-20490-8283/fema_p_361.pdf). Consult the FEMA Webpage "Safe Rooms," at http://www.fema.gov/safe-rooms, for more information and additional resources.

Explore options to retrofit roofing to secure it to the structure and increase resistance to high winds. Consult the FEMA Webpage "Protect Your Property from High Winds," at http://www.fema.gov/media-library/assets/documents/13270?id=3263, for more information.
Securely anchor rooftop equipment (e.g., HVAC units, lightning protection systems, antennae) to prevent them from detaching during high winds.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Design Guide for Improving Critical Facility Safety from Flooding and High Winds (FEMA 543), section 3.3.4, p. 3-81 to 3-97, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1557-20490-1542/fema543_complete.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Protect the facility's backup power generator and its fuel tank from high winds. Move the generator and tank inside or next to a building or protective structure to shield them from wind and windborne debris. Consult the FEMA flyer Install a Generator for Emergency Power, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf, for more information.

Explore the feasibility of procuring a backup power generator to power life safety systems in the event that a natural hazard disrupts power to the facility and its critical assets. Consult the FEMA flyer Install a Generator for Emergency Power, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf, for more information.
Install the necessary wiring and transfer switches to connect to a backup power generator and plan for its rapid deployment.
Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/tornadoes, for more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures during tornado season.

options.option_text: Establish tornado emergency plans and procedures. Contact the State/local emergency management office and/or the regional FEMA office for available tornado planning resources.
Conduct periodic, mandatory tornado drills for employees to practice tornado response procedures and condition themselves to react spontaneously and safely.

Develop shelter-in-place plans for facility occupants if it is not safe to evacuate the facility during a natural disaster. Train employees on the plan, and conduct recurring exercises on, reviews of, and updates to the plan. Ensure contractors and frequent visitors to the facility are aware of these plans. Consult the Ready.gov Website, available at http://www.ready.gov/shelter, for more information on sheltering.

Ensure all trees on facility property are far enough away to prevent them from damaging the facility if they should fall. Consult the FEMA flyer Remove Trees and Potential Windborne Missiles, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-3107/how2020_trees_missiles_4_11.pdf, for more information.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Remove Trees and Potential Windborne Missiles, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-3107/how2020_trees_missiles_4_11.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Keep limbs on nearby trees trimmed to prevent breakage. Falling tree limbs can damage facilities, assets, and critical utility lines.

If the facility does not have a backup generator, explore options to enter into a contract to have one readily available, in the event that a natural hazard disrupts power to the facility and its critical assets. Ensure that the facility is equipped (i.e., has the necessary wiring and transfer switches) to facilitate the rapid installation and operation of the generator unit.
Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/tornadoes, for more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate wildfire impacts.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of retrofitting the facility with fire-resistant materials such as tile, stucco, metal siding, brick, concrete block, and steel.
options.reference: Ready.gov, 2014, "Wildfires," April 9, http://www.ready.gov/wildfires, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Treat wood or combustible materials used in roofs, siding, decking, or trim with fire-retardant chemicals evaluated by a nationally recognized laboratory.
options.reference: Ready.gov, 2014, "Wildfires," April 9, http://www.ready.gov/wildfires, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of replacing skylights with fire-resistant-rated skylights and framing. Skylights are typically constructed with materials that can melt under intense heat.
options.reference: FEMA, 2008, Home Builder's Guide to Construction in Wildfire Zones: Technical Fact Sheet Series (FEMA P-737), "FS No. 10 – Windows and Skylights," September, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1652-20490-4085/fema_p_737.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Replace windows and glass doors with tempered safety glass.
options.reference: Nadel, B. A., 2004, Building Security Handbook for Architectural Planning and Design, New York, NY: McGraw-Hill, ch. 12, p. 12-18.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of installing fire-resistant door assemblies to protect the facility from fires.
options.reference: FEMA, 2008, Wildfire Hazard Mitigation Handbook for Public Facilities (FEMA P-754), section D.1, p. 31-32, October, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1715-25045-2934/fema_p_754.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Protect vent openings that may allow burning embers to enter the facility.
options.reference: FEMA, 2008, Wildfire Hazard Mitigation Handbook for Public Facilities (FEMA P-754), section C.1, p. 27-29, October, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1715-25045-2934/fema_p_754.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of constructing a noncombustible retaining wall to help increase setback.
options.reference: Texas Forest Service and Institute for Business & Home Safety, undated, Protect Your Property from Wildfire: Southwest Edition, p. 17, http://texasforestservice.tamu.edu/uploadedFiles/FRP/Predictive_Services/Prepare%20Your%20Home%20for%20Wildfire%20-%20Southwest%20Edition.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Design and landscape facility grounds home with wildfire safety in mind. Select materials and plants that are fire-resistant.
options.reference: Ready.gov, 2014, "Wildfires," April 9, http://www.ready.gov/wildfires, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Relocate propane tanks at least 30 feet from the facility, and create a 10-foot noncombustible zone around them.
options.reference: Texas Forest Service and Institute for Business & Home Safety, undated, Protect Your Property from Wildfire: Southwest Edition, p.15, http://texasforestservice.tamu.edu/uploadedFiles/FRP/Predictive_Services/Prepare%20Your%20Home%20for%20Wildfire%20-%20Southwest%20Edition.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/wildfires. For more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures for wildfires.

options.option_text: Establish specific plans and procedures to protect the facility and its critical assets from wildfires. Contact the State/local emergency management office and/or the regional FEMA office for available wildfire planning resources.

Conduct periodic, mandatory wildfire drills for employees to practice wildfire response procedures and condition themselves to react spontaneously and safely. Consult Prepare Your People: Wildfire Safety for Workplaces, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/1395779347101-00feb0d0db4bfdddd331a8026c09cbe8/20140318_PYP_WF_WP_508.pdf, for more information.
options.reference: America's PrepareAthon!, 2014, Prepare Your People: Wildfire Safety for Workplaces, March, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/1395779347101-00feb0d0db4bfdddd331a8026c09cbe8/20140318_PYP_WF_WP_508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Establish a liaison with the local fire department or forestry office to remain alert of hazardous conditions.
options.reference: FEMA/U.S. Fire Administration, 2004, Wildfire... Are you Prepared? (FEMA FA-287), August, http://www.usfa.fema.gov/downloads/pdf/publications/fa_287.pdf.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of adding shallow rooftop pools for firefighting during wildfire season.
options.reference: Nadel, B.A., 2004, Building Security: Handbook for Architectural Planning and Design, New York, NY: McGraw-Hill, ch. 12, p. 12-18.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of installing sprinklers, water storage tanks, and automated pumps with dedicated generators to coincide with wildfire season.
options.reference: Nadel, B.A., 2004, Building Security: Handbook for Architectural Planning and Design, New York, NY: McGraw-Hill, ch. 12, p. 12-18.

options.option_text: Establish a maintenance plan for irrigation systems, hoses, and generators.
options.reference: Nadel, B.A., 2004, Building Security: Handbook for Architectural Planning and Design, New York, NY: McGraw-Hill, ch. 12, p. 12-18.

options.option_text: Establish a maintenance plan to clear all flammable vegetation (e.g., dead branches, leaves, twigs) from facility grounds during wildfire season.
Keep a 30- to 100-foot safety zone around the facility during wildfire season where facility occupants could go to survive a wildfire in the vicinity.
options.reference: FEMA/U.S. Fire Administration, 2004, Wildfire... Are you Prepared? (FEMA FA-287), August, http://www.usfa.fema.gov/downloads/pdf/publications/fa_287.pdf.

options.option_text: Remove all wooden objects (e.g., shingles, non-essential equipment components) from the exterior of the facility.
options.reference: Nadel, B.A., 2004, Building Security: Handbook for Architectural Planning and Design, New York, NY: McGraw-Hill, ch. 12, p. 12-18.

options.option_text: Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/wildfires. For more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have deployable (i.e., non-permanent) mitigation measures to be put in place in anticipation of wildfires.

options.option_text: Establish the following plans and procedures in anticipation of an imminent wildfire:
options.reference: FEMA/U.S. Fire Administration, 2004, FA-287: Wildfire... Are you Prepared?, August, http://www.usfa.fema.gov/downloads/pdf/publications/fa_287.pdf.

options.option_text: If advised to evacuate, do so immediately.
options.reference: FEMA/U.S. Fire Administration, 2004, FA-287: Wildfire... Are you Prepared?, August, http://www.usfa.fema.gov/downloads/pdf/publications/fa_287.pdf.

options.option_text: Wet shrubs within 15 feet of the facility.
options.reference: FEMA/U.S. Fire Administration, 2004, FA-287: Wildfire... Are you Prepared?, August, http://www.usfa.fema.gov/downloads/pdf/publications/fa_287.pdf.

options.option_text: Shut down all utilities, if possible.
options.reference: FEMA/U.S. Fire Administration, 2004, FA-287: Wildfire... Are you Prepared?, August, http://www.usfa.fema.gov/downloads/pdf/publications/fa_287.pdf.

options.option_text: Shut off any natural gas, propane, or fuel oil supplies at the source.
Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/wildfires. For more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate the impacts of severe winter storms.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of retrofitting the facility to withstand snow loads and prevent roof collapse.
options.reference: FEMA, 2013, Mitigation Ideas: A Resource for Reducing Risks to Natural Hazards, p. 52, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1904-25045-0186/fema_mitigation_ideas_final508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Winterize the facility by insulating walls, caulking, and weather-stripping doors and windows, and installing storm windows or covering windows with plastic. Such measures can extend the life of the fuel supply.
Retrofit water pipes with enhanced insulation to prevent freezing.

Explore the feasibility of procuring a backup power generator to power life safety systems in the event that a natural hazard disrupts power to the facility and its critical assets. Consult the FEMA flyer Install a Generator for Emergency Power, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf, for more information.
Install the necessary wiring and transfer switches to connect to a backup power generator and plan for its rapid deployment.
For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/winter-weather.

vulnerability_text: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures for severe winter storms.

options.option_text: Establish specific plans and procedures for severe winter storms. Contact the State/local emergency management office and/or the regional FEMA office for available severe winter storm planning resources.

Develop shelter-in-place plans for facility occupants if it is not safe to evacuate the facility during a natural disaster. Train employees on the plan, and conduct recurring exercises on, reviews of, and updates to the plan. Ensure contractors and frequent visitors to the facility are aware of these plans. Consult the Ready.gov Website, available at http://www.ready.gov/shelter, for more information on sheltering.
Use snow fences or “living snow fences” (i.e., rows of trees or other vegetation) to limit blowing and drifting snow.
options.reference: FEMA, 2013, Mitigation Ideas: A Resource for Reducing Risks to Natural Hazards, p. 52, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1904-25045-0186/fema_mitigation_ideas_final508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Develop plans and procedures to sand areas to improve traction, spread rock salt to melt ice, and make snow removal equipment readily available.
Explore the feasibility of installing roadway heating technology to prevent ice/snow buildup.
options.reference: FEMA, 2013, Mitigation Ideas: A Resource for Reducing Risks to Natural Hazards, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1904-25045-0186/fema_mitigation_ideas_final508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Keep limbs on nearby trees trimmed to prevent breakage. Falling tree limbs can damage facilities, assets, and critical utility lines.

If the facility does not have a backup generator, explore options to enter into a contract to have one readily available, in the event that a natural hazard disrupts power to the facility and its critical assets. Ensure that the facility is equipped (i.e., has the necessary wiring and transfer switches) to facilitate the rapid installation and operation of the generator unit.
For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/winter-weather.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have deployable (i.e., non-permanent) mitigation measures to be put in place in anticipation of a severe winter storm.

options.option_text: Establish plans and procedures to shut off water pipes to prevent freezing and possibly bursting.
Insulate pipes with insulation or newspapers and plastic and allow faucets to drip slightly to avoid freezing.

Watch for warning signs of overstress conditions (e.g., sagging ceiling tiles, cracks in walls or masonry, and severe roof leaks) during events involving heavy snow. If conditions exist, evacuate personnel and request a structural inspection. Consult the FEMA flyer Snow Load Safety Guidance, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/1392984631969-ac57339deb6ee839a52b16b01eeee53e/FEMA_Snow_Load_508.pdf, for more information.
options.reference: FEMA, 2013, Risk Management Series – Snow Load Safety Guide (FEMA P-957), section 4.2, p. 4-3 to 4-4, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/7d8c55d1c4f815edf3d7e7d1c120383f/FEMA957_Snowload_508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Remove snow from the roof, if possible, following proper OSHA protocol for working on rooftops to ensure safety. Heavy snow accumulation can damage a facility's roof and possibly cause it to collapse.
options.reference: FEMA, 2013, Risk Management Series – Snow Load Safety Guide (FEMA P-957), section 5.3, p. 5-4 to 5-6, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/7d8c55d1c4f815edf3d7e7d1c120383f/FEMA957_Snowload_508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Inspect drains, gutters, downspouts, and vents for snow or ice blockage from earlier snowfalls to make room for new snow.
options.reference: FEMA, 2013, Risk Management Series – Snow Load Safety Guide (FEMA P-957), section 5.1, p. 5-2, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/7d8c55d1c4f815edf3d7e7d1c120383f/FEMA957_Snowload_508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/winter-weather.

vulnerability_text: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate the impacts of lightning strikes.

options.option_text: Install lightning protection devices and methods, such as lightning rods and grounding stakes/cables, on communications infrastructure and other critical facilities.
options.reference: FEMA, 2013, Mitigation Ideas: A Resource for Reducing Risks to Natural Hazards, p. 40, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1904-25045-0186/fema_mitigation_ideas_final508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Ensure that any installed lightning protection systems are attached properly and can withstand high winds that may accompany thunder storms. Consult the FEMA flyer Rooftop Attachment of Lightning Protection Systems in High-Wind Regions, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1605-20490-6583/fema549_apndx_e_ra7.pdf, for more information.
Install and maintain surge protection on critical electronic equipment.
options.reference: FEMA, 2013, Mitigation Ideas: A Resource for Reducing Risks to Natural Hazards, p. 40, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1904-25045-0186/fema_mitigation_ideas_final508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/thunderstorms-lightning, for more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures for lightning strikes.

options.option_text: Post signage around the facility to promote awareness of lightning dangers.
options.reference: FEMA, 2013, Mitigation Ideas: A Resource for Reducing Risks to Natural Hazards, p. 40, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1904-25045-0186/fema_mitigation_ideas_final508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Train employees on the dangers of lightning and what safety precautions to take if caught in a lightning storm.
options.reference: FEMA, 2013, Mitigation Ideas: A Resource for Reducing Risks to Natural Hazards, p. 40, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1904-25045-0186/fema_mitigation_ideas_final508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Keep limbs on nearby trees trimmed to prevent breakage. Falling tree limbs can damage facilities, assets, and critical utility lines.
Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/thunderstorms-lightning, for more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate the impacts of high winds (not associated with hurricanes/tornados).

options.option_text: Install permanent storm shutters to protect windows to help prevent glass breakage and stop windborne objects from penetrating the facility. Always use permanent storm shutters in areas where there will be little advance warning of high winds, as permanent shutters can be closed quickly, unlike temporary plywood covers that must be retrieved from storage and mounted with bolts or screws. Consult the FEMA flyer Protect Windows and Doors with Covers, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-8875/how2017_how2029_protect_windows_doors_4_11.pdf, for more information.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Protect Windows and Doors with Covers, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-8875/how2017_how2029_protect_windows_doors_4_11.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Explore options to retrofit roofing to secure it to the structure and increase resistance to high winds. Consult the FEMA Webpage "Protect Your Property from High Winds," at http://www.fema.gov/media-library/assets/documents/13270?id=3263, for more information.
Securely anchor rooftop equipment (e.g., HVAC units, lightning protection systems, antennae) to prevent them from detaching during high winds.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Design Guide for Improving Critical Facility Safety from Flooding and High Winds (FEMA 543), section 3.3.4, p. 3-81 to 3-97, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1557-20490-1542/fema543_complete.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Protect the facility's backup power generator and its fuel tank from high winds. Move the generator and tank inside or next to a building or protective structure to shield them from wind and windborne debris. Consult the FEMA flyer Install a Generator for Emergency Power, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf, for more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures for high winds (not associated with hurricanes/tornados).

options.option_text: Ensure all trees on facility property are far enough away to prevent them from damaging the facility if they should fall. Consult the FEMA flyer Remove Trees and Potential Windborne Missiles, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-3107/how2020_trees_missiles_4_11.pdf, for more information.
options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Remove Trees and Potential Windborne Missiles, April,  http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-3107/how2020_trees_missiles_4_11.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

options.option_text: Keep limbs on nearby trees trimmed to prevent breakage. Falling tree limbs can damage facilities, assets, and critical utility lines.

vulnerability_text: Vehicles can park within 400 feet of the facility. Access to the parking area is uncontrolled. The lack of rigorous vehicle access control procedures increases the vulnerability of the facility to a vehicle-borne attack.

options.option_text: Explore options to implement vehicle access control procedures within 400 feet of the facility. Options to consider include vehicle screening and locked vehicle access points.
options.reference: New York Police Department, 2009, Engineering Security: Protective Design for High Risk Buildings, ch. 5, http://www.nyc.gov/html/nypd/downloads/pdf/counterterrorism/nypd_engineeringsecurity_low_res.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

options.option_text: Explore options to restrict parking within 400 feet of the facility, to minimize consequences from potential VBIEDs.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-24, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: Locate drop-off zones at least 400 feet from the facility.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-24, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: Locate visitor or general public parking at least 400 feet from the facility.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-24, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: Allow only permit parking by authorized individuals of inspected vehicles within 400 feet of the facility.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-24, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: (1) Determine the facility’s susceptibility to blast. Consult Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, for more information, especially Chapters 2 and 3: Chapter 2 discusses blast design concerns and protective measures for areas surrounding buildings. Chapter 3 discusses the nature of explosive blasts, their effects on buildings and occupants, and the concept of levels of protection. In addition, review the DHS Bomb Threat Stand-off Chart, available through the Lessons Learned Information Sharing national, online network, at https://www.llis.dhs.gov/sites/default/files/DHS-BombThreatChart-6-5-09.pdf. (2) Based on the facility’s potential vulnerabilities, explore the feasibility of conducting a preliminary blast analysis using simple, commercially available building modeling and software designed to support the ability to address blast mitigation and potential VBIED threats, such as A.T.-BLAST by ARA Applied Research Associates. (3) Based on the results from the preliminary blast analysis, explore the feasibility of consulting with an engineering firm that specializes in structural fragility to procure a professional blast assessment to inform capital investments intended to protect personnel and the facility against blast.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-24, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

vulnerability_text: Uncontrolled parking is available adjacent to the facility, on the street.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of installing high curbs and other measures to keep vehicles from departing the roadway in an effort to avoid security countermeasures.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 6.2.1, p. 6-5, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of restricting or eliminating parking along the curb, and/or eliminating street-side loading zones for high-risk assets.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 2.5, p. 2-30, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: Request appropriate permits to restrict parking in curb lanes in densely populated areas to company-owned vehicles or key employee vehicles.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 6.7.2, p. 6-26, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of prohibiting street parking or enacting lane closure as a temporary measure during times of increased alert. Carefully plan such closures against elevated threats to avoid improvising with unaesthetic and disruptive measures.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 6.2.1, p. 6-5, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: (1) Determine the facility’s susceptibility to blast. Consult Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, for more information, especially Chapters 2 and 3: Chapter 2 discusses blast design concerns and protective measures for areas surrounding buildings. Chapter 3 discusses the nature of explosive blasts, their effects on buildings and occupants, and the concept of levels of protection. In addition, review the DHS Bomb Threat Stand-off Chart, available through the Lessons Learned Information Sharing national, online network, at https://www.llis.dhs.gov/sites/default/files/DHS-BombThreatChart-6-5-09.pdf. (2) Based on the facility’s potential vulnerabilities, explore the feasibility of conducting a preliminary blast analysis using simple, commercially available building modeling and software designed to support the ability to address blast mitigation and potential VBIED threats, such as A.T.-BLAST by ARA Applied Research Associates. (3) Based on the results from the preliminary blast analysis, explore the feasibility of consulting with an engineering firm that specializes in structural fragility to procure a professional blast assessment to inform capital investments intended to protect personnel and the facility against blast.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-24, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

vulnerability_text: Uncontrolled parking is available under the building.

options.option_text: Parking within the building leaves the facility highly susceptible to accidental or intentional damage, but if parking elsewhere is not an option, apply the following restrictions:
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-24, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: Permit parking only for company vehicles and employees of the building.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-24, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: Require proper credentials for all passengers.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-24, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: Conduct full vehicle inspections.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-24, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: Require ID checks for visitor parking.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-24, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: Ensure under-building parking is well-lighted and free of places of concealment and dead-end parking spaces.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 6.7.3, p. 6-29, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

vulnerability_text: Uncontrolled parking is available above the building.

options.option_text: Parking within the building leaves the facility highly susceptible to accidental or intentional damage, but if parking elsewhere is not an option, apply the following restrictions:
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-24, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: Permit parking only for company vehicles and employees of the building.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-24, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: Require proper credentials for all passengers.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-24, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: Conduct full vehicle inspections.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-24, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: Require ID checks for visitor parking.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-24, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: Maximize visibility for surveillance into, out of, and across the parking garage.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-25, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

vulnerability_text: Parking in the uncontrolled parking area is not monitored in any way.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of providing 24/7 CCTV monitoring for the parking area. Although CCTV coverage may need to be limited in its scope, it can still provide satisfactory coverage.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-23 to 5-25, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014; Fischer, R.J., E.P. Halibozek, and D.C. Walters, 2013, Introduction to Security, 9th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 206; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 166.

options.option_text: Install cameras in areas such as the main entrance, at all vehicle access control points, guard booths, cashier booths, and elevator lobbies on every floor. This will ensure more than adequate security.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-23 to 5-25, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014; Fischer, R.J., E.P. Halibozek, and D.C. Walters, 2013, Introduction to Security, 9th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 206; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 141-142.

options.option_text: Position the cameras to view the parking ramps and driveways as well as the intercom stations.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-23 to 5-25, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014; Fischer, R.J., E.P. Halibozek, and D.C. Walters, 2013, Introduction to Security, 9th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 206.

options.option_text: Ensure lighting adequately illuminates activity in the parking area for viewing and videotaping.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-25, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: Ensure that the parking area is covered by roving patrols conducted by the security force. Routes and times for patrols should also be varied at frequent intervals to preclude establishing a routine that may be observed by potential intruders.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 9-5, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014; FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), Appendix A, p. A-2 to A-3, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of using facility employees to monitor uncontrolled parking areas.

Provide suspicious activity awareness training to facility employees, including topics related to uncontrolled parking areas. Provide options (e.g., hotline, direct phone or radio communications with security operations) for employees to report suspicious activity, potential threats, and incidents.

vulnerability_text: There are no procedures or policies to identify and act on unauthorized vehicles parked in the uncontrolled parking area for an extended period.

options.option_text: Implement SOPs to address unauthorized, suspicious, and extended-stay vehicles. Processes to consider include reporting vehicles to security and/or local law enforcement, and contracting with an area towing company for vehicle removal.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 2.5, p. 2-30, section 3.3, p. 3-10 to 3-14, section 5.8, p. 5-23 to 5-25, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: Ensure that the parking area is covered by roving patrols conducted by the security force to ensure early identification of unauthorized parked vehicles.

options.option_text: Permit parking only for company vehicles and employees of the building.

vulnerability_text: Parking in the controlled parking area is not monitored in any way.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of providing 24/7 CCTV monitoring for the parking area. Although CCTV coverage may need to be limited in its scope, it can still provide satisfactory coverage.

Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 166.

options.option_text: Install cameras in areas such as the main entrance, at all vehicle access control points, guard booths, cashier booths, and elevator lobbies on every floor. This will ensure more than adequate security.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-23 to 5-25, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014; Fischer, R.J., E.P. options.option_text: Position the cameras to view the parking ramps and driveways as well as the intercom stations.
