vulnerability_text: During business hours, the controlled parking area is unattended. No personnel are involved in providing vehicle access control for the controlled parking area.

options.option_text: Explore options to staff the controlled parking area. If possible, employ security personnel to screen drivers and/or inspect vehicles.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.5.3, p. 5-11 to 5-14, section 5.8, p. 5-23 to 5-25, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: If SOPs for vehicle inspections are implemented, ensure that inspection areas are large enough to accommodate a minimum of one vehicle and a pull-out lane. They should also be covered and capable of accommodating the inspection of the undercarriage plus overhead inspection equipment. Inspection bays that can be closed to protect inspection equipment and staff in the event of bad weather are ideal.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.5.3, p. 5-12, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: Investigate available inspection technologies (e.g., above-vehicle and under-vehicle surveillance systems, ion scanning, and x-ray equipment) when sizing and designing vehicle inspection areas.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.5.3, p. 5-12, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

vulnerability_text: During business hours, the controlled parking area is attended by a ticket or toll agent. Although the attendant may be able to report unauthorized vehicle access or suspicious activity, security personnel staffing this control point could provide a more rapid response.

options.option_text: Evaluate the need to provide security staffing for vehicle access control points. Security force personnel may be trained and authorized to conduct vehicle searches and deny vehicle access.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 2.5, p. 2-30, section 5.5, p. 5-8 to 5-15, section 6.5, p. 6-22 to 6-23, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

vulnerability_text: During business hours, access to the controlled parking area is remotely controlled via a call button or camera without validation. Any vehicle, without validation of the driver's identification or the purpose of the driver's visit, can enter the facility.

options.option_text: Evaluate the need to provide security staffing for vehicle access control points. Security force personnel may be trained and authorized to conduct vehicle searches and deny vehicle access.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 2.5, p. 2-30, section 5.5, p. 5-8 to 5-15, section 6.5, p. 6-22 to 6-23, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

vulnerability_text: During business hours, vehicle searches are not conducted as vehicles enter the controlled parking area.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of executing searches on all vehicles entering the controlled parking area for the facility.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 2.5, p. 2-30, section 5.5, p. 5-8 to 5-15, section 6.5, p. 6-22 to 6-23, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: Execute random searches on vehicles entering the controlled parking area for the facility. Evaluate the need to conduct searches on all vehicles at elevated threat levels.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 2.5, p. 2-30, section 5.5, p. 5-8 to 5-15, section 6.5, p. 6-22 to 6-23, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

vulnerability_text: During business hours, locks or technology controls are not used to control access to the controlled parking area.

options.option_text: Employ a locking mechanism to help ensure that the controlled parking area remains secured during business hours.
options.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), Appendix A, p. A-3, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

options.option_text: Explore options to staff the controlled parking area. If possible, employ security personnel to screen drivers and/or inspect vehicles.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have a designated business continuity manager. The lack of a designated business continuity manager may hinder the implementation of business continuity policies, programs, or procedures.

options.option_text: Appoint or nominate a person with appropriate seniority and authority to be accountable for business continuity management policy and implementation. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/program-management.
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 5.2, p. 13.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not participate in emergency preparedness working groups.

options.option_text: Join relevant emergency preparedness working groups. Establish liaisons and regular communications with Federal, State, and local-level emergency preparedness working groups, as well as private sector/industry working groups, to share expertise and prepare to better respond to emergencies.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have a written business continuity plan.

options.option_text: Develop a comprehensive business continuity plan to be implemented in the aftermath of an incident. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/continuity.
options.reference: ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.4.4, p. 18, section 8.5, p. 19.

options.option_text: Develop a comprehensive business continuity plan to be implemented in the aftermath of an incident. The business continuity plan should contain the following elements: a) defined roles and responsibilities for personnel with authority during and following an incident; b) a process for activating the response; c) details to manage the immediate consequences of a disruptive incident (with regard to the welfare of individuals; strategic, tactical, and operational response options, and prevention of further loss or unavailability of prioritized activities); d) details on how and under what circumstances the facility will communicate with employees and their relatives, key interested parties, and emergency contacts; e) how the facility will continue or recover its prioritized activities within predetermined timeframes; f) details of the facility's media response following an incident; and g) a process for standing down once the incident is over. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/continuity.
options.reference: ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.4.4, p. 18, section 8.5, p. 19.

options.option_text: Develop a comprehensive business continuity plan to be implemented in the aftermath of an incident. The business continuity plan should identify stakeholders that need to be notified; critical and time-sensitive applications; alternative work sites; vital records, contact lists, processes, and functions that must be maintained; and personnel, procedures, and resources that are necessary while the facility recovers. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/continuity.
options.reference: NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section 6.9, p. 1600-9, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: For government agencies, a continuity of operations plan may be preferred to a business continuity plan. Develop a continuity of operations plan to document the overarching strategy, policies, and procedures required to support the organization's continuity of operations program. Visit the FEMA Website for related resources, at http://www.fema.gov/planning-templates.

Refer to the Business Continuity Planning Suite, developed by DHS’ National Protection and Programs Directorate and FEMA, for assistance with business continuity planning. This tool is scalable for use by organizations of any size and consists of a business continuity plan training; automated business continuity plan and disaster recovery plan generators; and a self-directed exercise for testing an implemented business continuity plan. The Business Continuity Planning Suite is available at http://www.ready.gov/business-continuity-planning-suite.

vulnerability_text: The corporate-level business continuity plan does not reflect facility-specific needs.

options.option_text: Develop a facility-specific addendum to the corporate business continuity plan. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/continuity.
Develop a comprehensive business continuity plan specific to the facility. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/continuity.

Refer to the Business Continuity Planning Suite, developed by DHS’ National Protection and Programs Directorate and FEMA, for assistance with business continuity planning. This tool is scalable for use by organizations of any size and consists of a business continuity plan training; automated business continuity plan and disaster recovery plan generators; and a self-directed exercise for testing an implemented business continuity plan. The Business Continuity Planning Suite is available at http://www.ready.gov/business-continuity-planning-suite.

vulnerability_text: Senior management personnel have not approved the facility's business continuity plan, which suggests a possible lack of buy-in or oversight of business continuity concerns.

options.option_text: Acquire the signature and approval of a senior manager or designated representative for the business continuity plan. The successful establishment of business continuity management is dependent upon its integration with strategic and day-to-day facility management and its alignment with business priorities. To be effective, business continuity management must be recognized from the outset by senior management. Business continuity management must always start at the top – for the essential reason that business continuity management is about what’s important and time-sensitive.
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 5, p. 13, section 6.7, p. 20; ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 5.2, p.10.

vulnerability_text: The facility has not coordinated the business continuity plan with stakeholders.

options.option_text: Coordinate the business continuity plan with stakeholders (e.g., customers, regulatory agencies).
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.8, p. 25; NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section 6.7.4, p. 1600-9, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: When determining appropriate business continuity management strategies, consider and protect the interests of key stakeholders. Identify appropriate strategies to manage relationships with key stakeholders, business or service partners, and contractors.
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.8, p. 25.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not review the business continuity plan annually with stakeholders.

options.option_text: Review the business continuity plan annually with stakeholders to ensure that business continuity management and the related business continuity plan provide an effective response that safeguards the interests of the facility's stakeholders.
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.8, p. 25.

vulnerability_text: Key personnel are not aware of, or do not have access to, the business continuity plan.

options.option_text: Ensure key personnel are aware of, and have a copy of, the business continuity plan.
options.reference: ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 7.3, p. 13.

options.option_text: Ensure that key personnel are aware of the business continuity plan, know their contributions to its effectiveness, the implications of not conforming, and their own role during disruptive incidents.
options.reference: ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 7.3, p. 13.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not train personnel on the business continuity plan.

options.option_text: Train all personnel on the business continuity plan annually. Document the completion of training.
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 5.3, p. 14, section 9.4, p. 37; ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 7.3, p. 13.

options.option_text: Provide training to the business continuity team on the roles and responsibilities as defined in the business continuity plan. Provide additional training for leaders including incident management.
options.reference: Ready.gov, 2012, "Training," November 1, http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training.

vulnerability_text: The facility trains some, but not all, personnel on the business continuity plan.

options.option_text: Train all personnel on the business continuity plan annually. Document the completion of training.
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 5.3, p. 14, section 9.4, p. 37; ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 7.3, p. 13.

options.option_text: Provide training to the business continuity team on the roles and responsibilities as defined in the business continuity plan. Provide additional training for leaders including incident management.
options.reference: Ready.gov, 2012, "Training," November 1, http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not exercise its business continuity plan annually.

options.option_text: Exercise and test the business continuity plan annually to ensure procedures are consistent with business continuity objectives. Use appropriate scenarios, and involve relevant interested parties. Produce post-exercise reports containing outcomes, recommendations, and actions to implement improvements.
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 9.3.4, p. 37; ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.5, p. 19.

options.option_text: Evaluate the business continuity plan through annual exercises. Ensure exercises are designed to accomplish the following: 1) Identify planning and procedural deficiencies; 2) Test or validate recently changed procedures or plans; 3) Clarify roles and responsibilities; 4) Obtain participant feedback and recommendations for program improvement; 5) Measure improvement compared to performance objectives; 6) Improve coordination between internal and external teams, organizations, and entities; 7) Validate training and education; and 8) Identify additional resources and assess the capabilities of existing resources, including personnel and equipment needed for effective business continuity and recovery.
options.reference: NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), ch. 8, p. 1600-10, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not document the results of business continuity plan exercises.

options.option_text: Document the results of business continuity plan exercises. Produce post-exercise reports containing outcomes, recommendations, and actions to implement improvements.
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 9.3, p. 36; ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.5, p. 19.

vulnerability_text:
The facility's business continuity plan is missing key elements.
Update the business continuity plan to include:

options.option_text: Update the business continuity plan to include:

Business continuity plan activation (i.e., the facility should establish and document clear guidelines and a set of criteria regarding which individual(s) have the authority to invoke the plan(s) and under what circumstances)
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 6.1.1, p. 16; ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.4.4, p. 18.

options.option_text: Immediate (operational) mitigation measures/strategies for responding to the disruption and prevent further loss
options.reference: ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.4.4, p. 18.

options.option_text: Interim (tactical) mitigation measures/strategies for responding to the disruption and prevent further loss
options.reference: ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.4.4, p. 18.

options.option_text: Long-term (strategic) mitigation measures/strategies for responding to the disruption and prevent further loss
options.reference: ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.4.4, p. 18.

options.option_text: Identification of pertinent risks and hazards (i.e., a comprehensive risk assessment that identifies the range of possible hazards, threats, or emergencies that have impacted or might impact the facility, surrounding area, or critical infrastructure supporting the facility)
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 5.5, p. 15; ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.2.3, p. 16; NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section A.5.2, p. 1600-14, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility's business continuity plan does not identify critical processes and assets necessary for core operations.

options.option_text: Identify critical processes and assets required to maintain continuity of operations.
options.reference: ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.2.2, p. 15; NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section A.5.3, p. 1600-15, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Identify critical processes and assets necessary for core operations to ensure that mission-critical activities are maintained during a disruptive event.
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 8.3.4, p. 29.

vulnerability_text:
The facility's business continuity plan identifies critical processes and assets necessary for core operations; however, the facility's impact evaluation for these processes and assets does not address some key impacts.
Update the impact evaluation, or, BIA, to consider the following:

options.option_text: Update the impact evaluation, or, BIA, to consider the following:
Customer service
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 6.2.3, p. 17.

options.option_text: Work backlog
options.reference: NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section A.5.3, p. 1600-16, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Third-party relationships and interdependencies
options.reference: ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.2.2, p. 15.

options.option_text: Regional, national and international considerations (i.e., the analysis shall evaluate the potential effects of regional, national, or international incidents that could have cascading impacts)
options.reference: NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section 5.2.4, p. 1600-7, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Health and safety of persons in the affected area
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 6.2.3, p. 17.

options.option_text: Regulatory and contractual obligations
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 6.2.3, p. 17.

options.option_text: Reputation or consumer confidence
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 6.2.3, p. 17.

options.option_text: Recovery Point Objectives
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.6, p. 24.

options.option_text: Recovery time objectives for each key product or service identified (if business continuity is the chosen strategy for a key product or service, a recovery time objective should be established and the continuity strategies should be evaluated against this objective)
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 6.3, p. 17; NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section A.5.2.3, p. 1600-15, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text:
The facility's business continuity plan is missing key elements.
Update the business continuity plan to include:

options.option_text: Update the business continuity plan to include:

Maximum Acceptable Outage, i.e., the maximum period of time that critical business processes can continue before the loss of critical resources affect their operations. This is the time it would take for adverse impacts, which might arise as result of not providing a product/service or performing an activity, to become unacceptable.
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 6.2.2, p. 17; ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.2.2, p. 15.

options.option_text: Trigger points that identify activation of plans, notification, or other actions, i.e., criteria that define when business continuity plans, policies, or procedures should be implemented to reduce the consequences of an event
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 6.6.5, p. 20.

options.option_text: An up-to-date point of contact roster
Key personnel responsible for continuity activities (e.g., organizational resilience or crisis management teams)
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 8.3.6, p. 29, section 8.5.6, p. 29; ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 7.4, p. 13, section 8.4.4, p. 18.

options.option_text: Essential infrastructure contacts (e.g., utilities, suppliers, providers)
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 8.3.6, p. 29; ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 7.4, p. 13.

options.option_text: An up-to-date point of contact roster for key personnel responsible for continuity activities (e.g., organizational resilience or crisis management teams)
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 8.3.6, p. 29, section 8.5.6, p. 29; ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 7.4, p. 13, section 8.4.4, p. 18.

options.option_text: An up-to-date point of contact roster for key personnel responsible for continuity activities (e.g., organizational resilience or crisis management teams)
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 8.3.6, p. 29; ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 7.4, p. 13.

options.option_text: Alert and notification procedures for communications with employees
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 8.5.4, p. 30; ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 7.4, p. 13, section 8.4.4, p. 18.

options.option_text: Identification of personnel with special skills, education, or training
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.3, p. 22.

options.option_text: Identification of alternates
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.3, p. 22.

options.option_text: Identification of alternates to personnel with special skills, education, or training
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.3, p. 22.

options.option_text: Location and relocation procedures
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.3, p. 22.

options.option_text: Safe close-down procedures
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.3, p. 22.

options.option_text: Adequate security/property protection if the facility is closed or relocated
options.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.3.3, p. 25, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Communication and coordination for continuity activities with other stakeholders (e.g., customers, regulatory agencies, emergency response agencies)
options.reference: ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 7.4, p. 13, section 8.4.3, p. 18.

options.option_text: Notification to suppliers/utility providers
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 8.3.6, p. 29, section 8.7.5, p. 34.

options.option_text: Alternative work arrangements (e.g., telecommuting or assignment to other corporate locations)/virtual office option
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.4, p. 22.

options.option_text: Designated crisis management center, EOC, or IMCC
options.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.4.7, p. 33, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 8.5.7, p. 32; NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section A.6.7.1.1, p. 1600-20, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Identification of key emergency personnel by position
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.3, p. 22, section 8.3.3, p. 28; ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 5.2, p. 11.

options.option_text: Identification of alternates
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.3, p. 22, section 8.3.3, p. 28.

options.option_text: Identification of alternate key emergency personnel
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.3, p. 22, section 8.3.3, p. 28.

options.option_text: IT recovery
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.5, p. 22; ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.3.2, p. 16.

options.option_text: Decision process for activation and relocation
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 8.2, p. 26, section 8.7.2, p. 33; ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.4.4, p. 18.

options.option_text: Exercising the plan
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 9.2, p. 35; ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.5, p. 19; NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), ch.8, p. 1600-10, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Alternate sources for customers (e.g., other corporate facilities or contracts with competitors)
options.reference: ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 4.3.2, p. 10.

options.option_text: Plan maintenance (e.g., review and revision)
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 5.4.2, p. 15; ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 9.3, p. 21.

options.option_text: Pandemic response
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 8.3.4, p. 29; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 9.

options.option_text: Human resource procedures (e.g., employee counseling, financial support, payroll)
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 8.7.3, p. 34; ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.4.4, p. 18.

options.option_text: Reconstitution of normal operations
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 8.2.5, p. 27.

options.option_text: Insurance program for acceptance/retention and transfer of risk
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), sections 6.6.3 and 6.6.4, p. 20.

options.option_text: Devolution (e.g., closing the original facility)
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 6.7, p. 20; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 31.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have an alternate site for continuity of business when the primary location is not accessible.

options.option_text: Establish an alternate site to continue business when the primary location is not accessible. Ensure there is sufficient distance between the alternate site and the facility so they are not likely to be affected by the same incident. Identify the location of the alternate site in the business continuity plan and define who should report to the site and under what conditions.
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.4, p. 22.

vulnerability_text:
The facility has an alternate site, but it lacks some key capabilities and/or characteristics that would enhance its capacity to facilitate continuity of business.
Explore the feasibility of upgrading the alternate site to include the following capabilities and/or characteristics:

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of upgrading the alternate site to include the following capabilities and/or characteristics:
Full capability, i.e., to continue all essential functions
Sufficient distance between the alternate site and the facility
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.4, p. 22, note 1.

options.option_text: Capability to perform essential functions quickly and for an extended period
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 15.

options.option_text: Reliable logistical support, services, and infrastructure systems (e.g., utilities and backup generator)
Adequate security systems
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 8.7.3, p. 34.

options.option_text: Communication support
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 8.7.3, p. 34.

options.option_text: Activation or use during exercises
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 10.2, p. 40-41.

options.option_text: Transportation support (e.g., sufficient parking)
options.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.4.7, p. 35, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 8.7.3, p. 34.

options.option_text: Sufficient computer equipment and software
options.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.4.7, p. 34, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 8.7.3, p. 34.

options.option_text: Access to vital files, records, and databases
options.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.4.7, p. 35, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.6, p. 24.

options.option_text: Sufficient space and equipment
options.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.4.7, p. 34-35, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.7.1, p. 24.

options.option_text: Alternate modes of obtaining supplies
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.7.1, p. 24.

options.option_text: Consideration for health, safety, and emotional well-being of personnel
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.4, p. 22, note 1; Pickren, A., 1999, “Business Continuity Planning: New Choices in Alternate Site Selection,” Disaster Recovery Journal, http://www.drj.com/drworld/content/w4_072.htm, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Limited or no dependencies in common with the primary site
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.5.2, p. 23.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have a designated emergency manager. The lack of a designated emergency manager may hinder the implementation of emergency management policies, programs, or procedures.

options.option_text: Designate an employee as emergency manager to develop, implement, and coordinate all emergency management-related activities.
options.reference: NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section 4.2, p. 1600-6, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 16.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have a written emergency operation/emergency action plan.

options.option_text: Develop a comprehensive emergency action plan specific to the facility. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/emergency, and consult Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans, available at http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 5.

options.option_text: Develop a comprehensive emergency operation/emergency action plan specific to the facility. The emergency operation/action plan should assign responsibilities for carrying out specific actions to protect people (including those with special needs), property, operations, and the environment in an emergency, and to provide incident stabilization. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/emergency, and consult Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans, available at http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf.
options.reference: NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section 6.8, p. 1600-9, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The corporate-level emergency operation/emergency action plan does not reflect facility-specific needs.

options.option_text: Develop a facility-specific addendum to the corporate emergency operation/emergency action plan. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/emergency, and consult Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans, available at http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf.

Develop a comprehensive emergency operation/emergency action plan specific to the facility. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/emergency, and consult Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans, available at http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf.

vulnerability_text: Senior management personnel have not approved the facility's emergency operation/emergency action plan, which suggests a possible lack of buy-in or oversight of emergency concerns.

options.option_text: Acquire the signature and approval of a senior manager or designated representative for the emergency operation/emergency action plan.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 11.

vulnerability_text: The facility has not coordinated the emergency operation/emergency action plan with emergency responders.

options.option_text: Coordinate the emergency operation/emergency action plan with first responders. Establish MOUs/MOAs as necessary.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 23.

options.option_text: Share critical information about the facility (e.g., floor plans, location of emergency equipment, notification and contact lists) with emergency responders. Consult Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans, available at http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, for more information.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 23.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not review the emergency operation/emergency action plan annually with emergency responders.

options.option_text: Review the emergency operation/emergency action plan annually with emergency responders, to ensure these agencies are familiar with the facility and its emergency response plans, policies, and procedures.

Develop and implement procedures to review the emergency operation/emergency action plan, especially any revisions that have been implemented within the past year, with emergency responders on an annual basis.
Provide a copy of the emergency operation/emergency action to the appropriate emergency response agencies.

vulnerability_text: Key personnel are not aware of, or do not have access to, the emergency operation/emergency action plan.

options.option_text: Ensure key personnel are aware of, and have a copy of, the emergency operation/emergency action plan.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 16.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not train personnel on the emergency operation/emergency action plan.

options.option_text: Train all personnel on the emergency operation/emergency action plan at least annually. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training.
options.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.0, p. 19, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), ch. 7, p. 1600-9 to 1600-10, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Train all personnel on emergency procedures for life safety (e.g., evacuation, shelter, shelter-in-place and lockdown). Train members of the emergency response team so they are familiar with their role and responsibilities as defined within the emergency operation/emergency action plan. Provide advanced training to team leaders, including incident command system training, so they can effectively lead their teams. Maintain records documenting the scope of training, participants, instructors, and duration.
options.reference: Ready.gov, 2012, "Training," November 1, http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Provide training to obtain and maintain certifications to those emergency response team members that administer First Aid or CPR, or use AEDs.
options.reference: Ready.gov, 2012, "Training," November 1, http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Provide training in accordance with the applicable OSHA regulation for those employees who use portable fire extinguishers, fire hoses, or other firefighting equipment.
options.reference: Ready.gov, 2012, "Training," November 1, http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Provide appropriate training to employees who respond to HAZMAT spills.
options.reference: Ready.gov, 2012, "Training," November 1, http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility trains some, but not all, personnel on the emergency action/emergency operations plan.

options.option_text: Train all personnel on emergency procedures for life safety at least once a year.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not exercise its emergency operation/emergency action plan annually.

options.option_text: Conduct regular drills and exercises to validate the emergency operation/emergency action plan and to evaluate the ability of personnel to carry out their assigned roles and responsibilities. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/testing/exercises.
options.reference: NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), ch. 8, p. 1600-10, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014; Ready.gov, 2012, "Training," November 1, http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Review protective actions for life safety and conduct evacuation drills. Conduct sheltering and lockdown drills.
options.reference: NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), ch. 8, p. 1600-10, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014; Ready.gov, 2012, "Training," November 1, http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Evaluate the emergency operation/emergency action plan through annual exercises. Ensure exercises are designed to accomplish the following: 1) Identify planning and procedural deficiencies; 2) Test or validate recently changed procedures or plans; 3) Clarify roles and responsibilities; 4) Obtain participant feedback and recommendations for program improvement; 5) Measure improvement compared to performance objectives; 6) Improve coordination between internal and external teams, organizations, and entities; 7) Validate training and education; 8) Increase awareness and understanding of hazards and the potential impacts of hazards on the facility; and 9) Identify additional resources and assess the capabilities of existing resources, including personnel and equipment needed for effective response.
options.reference: NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), ch. 8, p. 1600-10, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Document all exercise results. Critically evaluate exercises to assess whether goals and objectives were achieved, the effectiveness of participation, and whether the emergency response management system will function as anticipated in the case of a real crisis. Modify the emergency operation/emergency action plan as necessary.
options.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.5.3, p. 37, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Consult the Homeland Security Exercise and Evaluation Program Website, at https://www.llis.dhs.gov/HSEEP, for a set of fundamental principles that frame a common approach to exercises, among other helpful resources.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not document the results of emergency operation/emergency action plan exercises.

options.option_text: Document the results of emergency operation/emergency action plan exercises. Implement procedures for capturing and implementing lessons learned. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training.
options.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 9.3.2, p. 36.

Changes in the hazard environment
Increased communications and notifications

options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 9.

options.option_text: Develop a response plan and training program to provide guidance on how to respond to an active shooter. Refer to the DHS Website for resources, including online training, booklets, and posters, at http://www.dhs.gov/active-shooter-preparedness.
Develop an active shooter protocol in close coordination with local first responders.
Provide initial and annual recurring active shooter training for all personnel.
Review and exercise the active shooter plan annually with all personnel.
Designate “safe rooms” throughout the facility. Ensure each room can be locked from within and has a telephone for communications with first responders.
Provide local first responders with a copy of the facility's floor plans to enhance their response capability in an active shooter incident.
Provide local first responders with a tour of the facility to familiarize them with its layout.
Internal disturbances (e.g., workplace violence)
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 9.

options.option_text: Develop a protocol to respond to workplace violence. Refer to the FBI Website for resources, such as the January 2011 FBI Law Enforcement Bulletin: Workplace Violence Prevention, available at http://www.fbi.gov/stats-services/publications/law-enforcement-bulletin/january2011/january_2011.
Provide training on workplace violence to all personnel at initial hire and annually thereafter.

Refer to the FEMA Emergency Management Institute's Independent Study Program training course IS-906: Workplace Security Awareness. This free, 1-hour course provides guidance to individuals and organizations on how to improve security in the workplace and is available at http://training.fema.gov/EMIWeb/IS/IS906.asp.
Hostage situations
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 9.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not exchange information with a State or local EOC.

options.option_text: Collaborate with a State and/or local EOC  to facilitate the exchange of real-time situational awareness information.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have an IMCC. Without an IMCC, the facility lacks a dedicated area to direct and oversee emergency management activities.

options.option_text: Establish an IMCC dedicated to facilitating the coordination and support of emergency management activities.
options.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.4.7, p. 33, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section 6.7.1.1, p. 1600-9, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 20.

options.option_text: Implement access control measures for the IMCC. Provide members of all teams who staff the IMCC with 24/7 access.
options.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.4.7, p. 33, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Ensure the IMCC has an uninterruptible power supply; essential computer, telecommunications, heating/ventilating/air conditioning systems; and other support systems to continue operations for at least 72 hours. In addition, identify and store emergency supplies in the IMCC.
options.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.4.7, p. 33, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

options.option_text: Determine the feasibility of including the following elements as part of the IMCC: sleeping quarters, dining/food preparation space, portable restrooms, briefing areas, communication area, and proper equipment and backup. Include these elements as appropriate. Ensure there is adequate parking.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 21.

options.option_text: Locate the IMCC offsite to ensure emergency management facilities are available and not impacted by an event at the facility.
options.reference: ASIS International, 2009, "Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009)," American National Standard, section A.4.7 (m) (iii), p. 33,  http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014. ; NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section A.6.7.1.1.1, p. 1600-20, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility's IMCC is located onsite. An event that impacts the facility may also make the IMCC unavailable for crisis management.

options.option_text: Relocate the IMCC to an offsite location to ensure emergency management facilities are available and not impacted by an event at the facility.
options.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.4.7, p. 33, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section A.6.7.1.1.1, p. 1600-20, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The primary IMCC has not been activated in the previous year through an exercise or an event. Annual activation of the IMCC would allow the facility to evaluate its ability to respond rapidly and effectively to emergencies and identify gaps in planning, training, and resources.

options.option_text: Activate the IMCC annually, and complete an after-action report following the activation.

vulnerability_text: The facility did not complete an after-action report following the activation of its IMCC.

options.option_text: Complete an after-action report following each activation of the IMCC and implement procedures for capturing and implementing lessons learned.

vulnerability_text: The IMCC cannot operate independently of all outside utilities for at least 72 hours.

options.option_text: Investigate ways to make the IMCC capable of operating independently of all outside utilities for at least 72 hours.
options.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.4.7, p. 33, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section A.6.7.1.1, p. 1600-20, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Ensure the IMCC has an uninterruptible power supply; essential computer, telecommunications, heating/ventilating/air conditioning systems; and other support systems to continue operations for at least 72 hours.
options.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.4.7, p. 33, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section A.6.7.1.1, p. 1600-20, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have a backup IMCC. If the primary IMCC was damaged, destroyed, or became inaccessible, the facility would not have a dedicated area to direct and oversee emergency management activities.

options.option_text: Establish an alternate IMCC to ensure emergency management facilities are available in the event of the loss of the primary IMCC.
options.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.4.7, p. 33, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section A.6.7.1.1.1, p. 1600-20, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 21.

vulnerability_text: The backup IMCC is not geographically separated from the primary IMCC site. An event could make the primary and backup IMCCs unavailable for crisis management.

options.option_text: Relocate the backup IMCC to ensure it remains available if an event impacts the primary IMCC and makes it unavailable for use.
options.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.4.7, p. 33, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section A.6.7.1.1.1, p. 1600-20, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 21.

vulnerability_text: The backup IMCC cannot operate independently of all outside utilities for at least 72 hours.

options.option_text: Investigate ways to make the backup IMCC capable of operating independently of all outside utilities for at least 72 hours.
options.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.4.7, p. 33, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), sections A.6.7.1.1 and A.6.7.1.1.1, p. 1600-20, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

options.option_text: Ensure the backup IMCC has an uninterruptible power supply; essential computer, telecommunications, heating/ventilating/air conditioning systems; and other support systems to continue operations for at least 72 hours.
options.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.4.7, p. 33, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), sections A.6.7.1.1 and A.6.7.1.1.1, p. 1600-20, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility security plan does not utilize a threat level system to guide the implementation of increased protective measures as threat levels increase.

options.option_text: Incorporate a threat level system into the security plan.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, Appendix C, p. C-1 to C-4, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

options.option_text: Incorporate the DHS NTAS into the security plan. The NTAS provides information timely, detailed information about terrorist threats to the public, government agencies, first responders, airports and other transportation hubs, and the private sector. Consult the DHS NTAS Website, at http://www.dhs.gov/national-terrorism-advisory-system, for more information.

Incorporate MARSEC levels into the security plan. MARSEC is a three-tiered system employed by the USGS to communicate with maritime industry partners of potential pre-planned responses to threats. MARSEC levels adjust in conjunction with NTAS alerts. Consult the USGS MARSEC Levels Website, at http://www.uscg.mil/safetylevels/whatismarsec.asp, for more information.
Incorporate an industry threat level system into the security plan.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not implement additional protective measures during elevated threats situations, which may leave the facility open to attack and significant damage.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of employing and implementing additional protective measures to increase the overall security posture of the facility as threat levels increase. Options may include additional access controls; barriers; increased communications and notification; enhanced cyber security; additional infrastructure upgrades and redundancy; enhanced incident response; additional monitoring and surveillance detection; and planning and preparedness.

Conduct planning and coordination and gather resources to implement additional protective measures appropriate for the facility during elevated threat situations in a timely manner. Train facility personnel on their responsibilities associated with these protective measures, and exercise these measures annually to evaluate facility preparedness and program effectiveness.

vulnerability_text:
The facility implements different protective measures during elevated threat situations, but additional protective measures would increase the overall security posture of the facility as threat levels increase.

Employ the following additional protective measures during elevated threat situations:

options.option_text: Employ the following additional protective measures during elevated threat situations:
Establish additional access controls
Restrict access to essential personnel only

options.option_text: Decrease the number of personnel authorized to be onsite
Prevent onsite access by visitors
