vulnerability_text: The facility does not have a security force. The lack of an adequately sized, well-trained, and well-equipped security force limits a facility's ability to respond to security incidents and/or emergencies.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of establishing or contracting a security force to actively protect assets, property, and employees, and to rapidly respond to security incidents and/or emergencies. Depending on State and local regulations, security personnel may be subject to registration, licensing, certification, specific training, and other requirements.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 3-7, p. 9-5 to 9-7, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

options.option_text: Ensure that the size of the security force allows for adequate staffing of static posts and roving patrols.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 3-6 to 3-7, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

options.option_text: Ensure that the security force has the equipment necessary to meet the requirements established by security management (e.g., uniforms, communications, personal protective equipment).
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Officer Operations, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 17.

options.option_text: Ensure that the security force receives adequate, continuous training on topics such as emergency response, weapons and self-defense, SOPs, and screening and access.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 9-6, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

options.option_text: Provide the security force with comprehensive post orders and a dedicated command and control/operation center.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 9-6, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

options.option_text: Create a surge capacity plan to augment the security force during special circumstances or elevate threats.
options.reference: Fischer, R.J., and G. Green, 2004, Introduction to Security, 7th Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 48-53.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have a security force. Employees perform security-related duties such as patrols and security checks. The lack of an adequately sized, well-trained, and well-equipped security force limits a facility's ability to respond to security incidents and/or emergencies.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of establishing or contracting a security force to actively protect assets, property, and employees, and to rapidly respond to security incidents and/or emergencies. Depending on State and local regulations, security personnel may be subject to registration, licensing, certification, specific training, and other requirements.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 3-7, p. 9-5 to 9-7, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

options.option_text: Ensure that the size of the security force allows for adequate staffing of static posts and roving patrols.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 3-6 to 3-7, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

options.option_text: Ensure that the security force has the equipment necessary to meet the requirements established by security management (e.g., uniforms, communications, personal protective equipment).
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Officer Operations, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 17.

options.option_text: Ensure that the security force receives adequate, continuous training on topics such as emergency response, weapons and self-defense, SOPs, and screening and access.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 9-6, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

options.option_text: Provide the security force with comprehensive post orders and a dedicated command and control/operation center.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 3-8, p. 9-6, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

options.option_text: Create a surge capacity plan to augment the security force during special circumstances or elevate threats.
options.reference: Fischer, R.J., and G. Green, 2004, Introduction to Security, 7th Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 48-53.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have a surge capacity plan to provide additional security personnel during special circumstances or elevated threat.

options.option_text: Establish a surge capacity plan to augment the security force during special circumstances, for example, during special events or natural disasters, and elevated threat situations. Options may include law enforcement officers (MOA, contract, and/or off-duty) or contract security (as part of an existing contract and/or another contract). Detail in the surge capacity plan the roles, responsibilities, and chain of command for both regular and surge forces.
options.reference: Fischer, R.J., and G. Green, 2004, Introduction to Security, 7th Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 48-53.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have an onsite security force. The company/organization employs a security force, but security personnel are not stationed at the facility. This limits the facility's ability to rapidly respond to security incidents and/or emergencies as they occur at the facility.

options.option_text: Explore the feasibility of extending the security force to provide a continuous, onsite presence at the facility to actively protect assets, property, and employees, and to rapidly respond to security incidents and/or emergencies.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 3-7, p. 9-5 to 9-7, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

options.option_text: Establish a surge capacity plan to augment the security force during special circumstances, for example, during special events or natural disasters, and elevated threat situations. Options may include law enforcement officers (MOA, contract, and/or off-duty) or contract security (as part of an existing contract and/or another contract). Detail in the surge capacity plan the roles, responsibilities, and chain of command for both regular and surge forces.
options.reference: Fischer, R.J., and G. Green, 2004, Introduction to Security, 7th Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 48-53.

vulnerability_text: The security force does not have the authority to temporarily detain persons suspected of criminal activity.

options.option_text: Evaluate the facility's security requirements. If detainment authority is necessary, seek appropriate consultation, for example, with your legal department, contracted security firm, or local law enforcement for further guidance.
options.reference: Fischer, R.J., G. Green, 2004, Introduction to Security, 7th Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 48.

vulnerability_text: The security force does not staff static posts at the facility's SAAs or other areas where entry control, monitoring, and/or protection is necessary (e.g., main entrance, loading dock).

options.option_text: Post security personnel at assets and areas where a continuous security force presence is necessary.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 3-7, p. 9-5 to 9-7, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

options.option_text: Provide special instructions for each post, such as duties, staffing hours, and required equipment.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 3-8, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

options.option_text: Establish a designated method of relief for security personnel assigned to static posts.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 9-5, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The security force does not staff static posts at most of the facility's SAAs or other areas where entry control, monitoring, and/or protection is necessary.

options.option_text: Explore ways to provide a continuous security force presence at the majority of the facility's SAAs and other areas as necessary.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 3-7, p. 9-5 to 9-7, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

options.option_text: Explore ways to provide a continuous security force presence at the majority of the facility's SAAs and other areas as necessary.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 3-7, p. 9-5 to 9-7, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The duration of security force staffing at static posts is limited.

options.option_text: Determine if the duration of security force staffing at static posts meets the facility's security requirements. Extend shifts at static posts as necessary.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 9-5, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

options.option_text: Determine if the duration of security force staffing at static posts meets the facility's security requirements. Extend shifts at static posts as necessary.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 9-5, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The security force does not conduct roving patrols that cover the facility's SAAs or other areas where monitoring and protection is necessary. If performed, roving patrols would allow the security force to more effectively monitor critical assets and areas, and deter, report, and respond to incidents.

options.option_text: Ensure that SAAs and other areas as necessary are covered by roving patrols conducted by the security force. Routes and times for patrols should also be varied at frequent intervals to preclude establishing a routine that may be observed by potential intruders.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 9-5, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The security force does not conduct random roving patrols. Security patrols that are conducted in a regular, unvarying sequence may be observed and exploited.

options.option_text: Vary routes and times of roving patrols at frequent intervals to preclude establishing a routine that may be observed by potential intruders.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 9-5, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The security force does not conduct roving patrols that cover most of the facility's SAAs or other areas where entry control, monitoring, and/or protection is necessary.

options.option_text: Explore ways to provide roving patrols for a majority of the facility's SAAs and other areas as necessary.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 9-5, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

options.option_text: Explore ways to provide roving patrols for a majority of the facility's SAAs and other areas as necessary.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 9-5, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The frequency of roving patrols is limited; therefore, detection and incident response at facility SAAs and other critical areas may be delayed.

options.option_text: Determine if the frequency of roving patrols meets the facility's security requirements for SAAs and other critical areas. Increase the  frequency of patrols as necessary.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 9-5, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

options.option_text: Determine if the frequency of roving patrols meets the facility's security requirements for SAAs and other critical areas. Increase the  frequency of patrols as necessary.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 9-5, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The security force does not wear uniforms. An employee or security officer in uniform provides a visible security presence.

options.option_text: Provide recognizable security uniforms to the guard force.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Officer Operations, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 17.

vulnerability_text: For special events, the facility does not use canines trained to detect IEDs to sweep protected area.

options.option_text: Coordinate with local, State, and/or Federal agencies (such as, local or State law enforcement, Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms, and Explosives) regarding the use of their explosive detection canines for sweeps.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 1-4, p. 7-7, p. 9-4, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The security force does not have communications devices. Communications equipment is essential for the safe and efficient operation of the security force and the success of its assigned mission.

options.option_text: Equip the security force with the necessary communication devices to carry out their roles and responsibilities.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Officer Operations, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 17.

options.option_text: Provide two-way radios with secure-voice capability, if possible, to the security force.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Officer Operations, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 17.

options.option_text: Provide additional communications equipment to the security force, such as cell phones, duress alarms, and/or panic buttons.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 3-5, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The security force does not receive any type of training. A robust security force training program can decrease mistakes, increase proficiency, and enhance a facility's overall security posture.

options.option_text: Develop or contract a formal training program for the security force that covers topics such as emergency response, SOPs (for example, facility-specific SOPs, ICS/NIMS), screening and access, and, if appropriate, weapons and self-defense. Continuation/in-service training should be provided to maintain and enhance job proficiency. Document training and retain training records.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 9-6, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

options.option_text: Employ security professionals certified or licensed through a Federal or State program. Ensure the training requirements they fulfilled to become certified/licensed will equip them to fulfill the facility's security requirements.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 9-6, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

vulnerability_text: Although the security force receives some form of training, it is not provided through an established program.

options.option_text: Develop or contract a formal training program for the security force that covers topics such as emergency response, SOPs (for example, facility-specific SOPs, ICS/NIMS), screening and access, and, if appropriate, weapons and self-defense. Continuation/in-service training should be provided to maintain and enhance job proficiency. Document training and retain training records.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 9-6, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

options.option_text: Employ security professionals certified or licensed through a Federal or State program. Ensure the training requirements they fulfilled to become certified/licensed will equip them to fulfill the facility's security requirements.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 9-6, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The security force does not receive continuous/in-service training. Refresher training helps security force personnel maintain continued proficiency and current knowledge of security issues, trends, and technologies.

options.option_text: Provide or contract refresher training for the security force, at least annually. Document refresher training and retain training records.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Officer Operations, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 92-93.

vulnerability_text: The security force does not receive training on emergency response procedures for bomb threats.

options.option_text: Ensure the security force receives training on emergency response procedures to bomb threats.
For information about bombing prevention training available through the DHS Office of Bombing Prevention, visit https://www.dhs.gov/bombing-prevention-training.
Consult with your local PSA for more information on a variety of related training and resources that are available from the DHS Office of Bombing Prevention.
Consult local and State law enforcement agencies' Websites that may provide information and training on emergency preparedness and response to mass casualty events, including IED attacks.

vulnerability_text: The security force does not receive training on emergency response procedures for break-ins.

options.option_text: Ensure the security force receives training on emergency response procedures for break-ins.

vulnerability_text: The security force does not receive training on emergency response procedures for hostage/barricade situations.

options.option_text: Ensure the security force receives training on emergency response procedures for hostage/barricade situations.

vulnerability_text: The security force does not receive training on emergency response procedures for fires.

options.option_text: Ensure the security force receives training on emergency response procedures for fires.

vulnerability_text: The security force does not receive training on emergency response procedures in the event of a HAZMAT release.

options.option_text: Ensure the security force receives training on emergency response procedures for a HAZMAT release.
Review the list of FEMA training materials available at http://www.training.fema.gov.

vulnerability_text: The security force does not receive training on procedures in the event of a natural disaster.

options.option_text: Ensure the security force receives training on emergency response procedures for regional natural disasters.
Review the list of FEMA training materials available at http://www.training.fema.gov.

vulnerability_text: The security force does not receive training on CPR/First Aid.

options.option_text: Ensure the security force receives training on CPR/First Aid.
Review the training materials available from the Red Cross at www.redcross.org/take-a-class.

vulnerability_text: The security force does not receive training on emergency response procedures for active shooter attacks.

options.option_text: Ensure the security force receives training on how to respond to an active shooter. Refer to the DHS Website for more information on active shooter response training courses, materials, and workshops available through various agencies, at http://www.dhs.gov/active-shooter-preparednes.
options.reference: DHS, 2008, Active Shooter – How to Respond, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/active_shooter_booklet.pdf, accessed July 10, 2014.

options.option_text: Develop active shooter response protocols in close coordination with local first responders.
options.reference: DHS, 2008, Active Shooter – How to Respond, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/active_shooter_booklet.pdf, accessed July 10, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The security force does not receive training on IED recognition.

options.option_text: Ensure the security force receives training on IED recognition. Consult the Ready.gov Website for more information, at http://www.ready.gov/explosions.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 9-6, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

options.option_text: For information about bombing prevention training available through the DHS Office of Bombing Prevention, visit https://www.dhs.gov/bombing-prevention-training.
Consult with your local PSA for more information on a variety of related training and resources that are available from the DHS Office of Bombing Prevention.
Consult local and State law enforcement agencies' websites that may provide information and training on emergency preparedness and response to mass casualty events, including IED attacks.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have comprehensive post orders for the security force, which may result in situations where security personnel do not fulfill their duties and/or perform outside the scope of their duties.

options.option_text: Develop comprehensive post orders and make them available at each guard station.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 3-8, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

options.option_text: Develop post orders that describe in detail the duties and responsibilities of the security force. After comprehensive post orders are developed, ensure that they are kept current and accessible. Comprehensive post orders may serve to express the policies of the protected enterprise, summarize required officer duties, avoid problems associated with of word-of-mouth instructions, and provide a basis for site-specific training. They are the vital link between the requirements of the facility and the ability of security personnel to effectively meet those requirements.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 3-8, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The security force does not have a dedicated command and control/operation center. Without a dedicated security center, the security force lacks a control point for coordination, monitoring and dispatch, and other critical activities.

options.option_text: Establish a command and control post/operation center for the security force.
options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 6-14, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have a designated security manager. The lack of a security manager may hinder the application of security policies, programs, or procedures.

options.option_text: Designate an employee to act as a security manager and task that person with developing, implementing, and coordinating all security-related activities. If possible, choose an employee with previous security experience. Consult The Critical Success Factor Method: Establishing a Foundation for Enterprise Security Management, available at http://www.ready.gov/document/critical-success-factor-method-establishing-foundation-enterprise-security-management-0, for more information.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have a written security plan.

options.option_text: Develop a comprehensive security plan specific to the facility. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year. Consult The Critical Success Factor Method: Establishing a Foundation for Enterprise Security Management, available at http://www.ready.gov/document/critical-success-factor-method-establishing-foundation-enterprise-security-management-0, for more information.
options.reference: Garcia, M.L., 2008, The Design and Evaluation of Physical Protection Systems, 2nd Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc.

options.option_text: Develop a comprehensive security plan specific to the facility. The plan should address issues such as the following: protection of employees, contractors/vendors, visitors, and executives; protection of sensitive information; protection of funds; facility access control procedures; suspicious activity reporting procedures; employee termination procedures; parking; mail security; background checks; prohibited items; security force; end-of-day security checks; control and accountability of equipment (including keys); electronic security systems (including CCTV); physical security inspection programs; and employee security awareness training programs. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year.

Develop a comprehensive security plan specific to the facility. The plan should address topics such as the following: physical security measures and systems, security force, access control procedures, information protection, and security awareness training. In addition, the plan should include elements such as the following: an assessment of possible security risks; a review of threats to the facility and the facility's vulnerabilities; an up-to-date point of contact roster for key personnel responsible for security and first responders; and identification of critical assets or areas. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year.

Consult The Critical Success Factor Method: Establishing a Foundation for Enterprise Security Management, available at http://www.ready.gov/document/critical-success-factor-method-establishing-foundation-enterprise-security-management-0, for more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have a written security plan. Although the facility may have documentation that addresses security policies, programs, and/or procedures, it does not have a separate, comprehensive security plan.

options.option_text: Develop a comprehensive security plan specific to the facility. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year.

Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year.

vulnerability_text: The corporate-level security plan does not reflect facility-specific needs.

options.option_text: Develop a facility-specific addendum to the corporate security plan.
options.reference: Garcia, M.L., 2008, The Design and Evaluation of Physical Protection Systems, 2nd Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc.; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Develop a comprehensive security plan specific to the facility.
options.reference: Garcia, M.L., 2008, The Design and Evaluation of Physical Protection Systems, 2nd Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc.; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Consult The Critical Success Factor Method: Establishing a Foundation for Enterprise Security Management, available at http://www.ready.gov/document/critical-success-factor-method-establishing-foundation-enterprise-security-management-0, for more information.

vulnerability_text: Senior management personnel have not approved the facility's security plan, which suggests a possible lack of buy-in or oversight of security concerns.

options.option_text: Acquire the signature and approval of a senior manager or designated representative for the security plan. Consult The Critical Success Factor Method: Establishing a Foundation for Enterprise Security Management, available at http://www.ready.gov/document/critical-success-factor-method-establishing-foundation-enterprise-security-management-0, for more information.
options.reference: Garcia, M.L., 2008, The Design and Evaluation of Physical Protection Systems, 2nd Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc.; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

vulnerability_text: The facility has not coordinated the security plan with local law enforcement.

options.option_text: Coordinate the security plan with local law enforcement. Consult The Critical Success Factor Method: Establishing a Foundation for Enterprise Security Management, available at http://www.ready.gov/document/critical-success-factor-method-establishing-foundation-enterprise-security-management-0, for more information.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Coordinate the security plan with local law enforcement, and establish mutual-aid agreements with appropriate response agencies. Consult The Critical Success Factor Method: Establishing a Foundation for Enterprise Security Management, available at http://www.ready.gov/document/critical-success-factor-method-establishing-foundation-enterprise-security-management-0, for more information.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Establish a liaison and regular communication with local law enforcement and other first responders, State and Federal law enforcement and counterterrorism agencies, and industry organizations to engage in information sharing, clarify response actions, track threat conditions, and support investigations.
options.reference: Garcia, M.L., 2008, The Design and Evaluation of Physical Protection Systems, 2nd Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc.; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Share critical information about the facility (e.g., floor plans, the location of critical assets or areas, notification and contact lists) with local law enforcement and emergency responders.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Consult Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans, available at http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, for more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not review the security plan annually with local law enforcement.

options.option_text: Review the security plan annually with local law enforcement and other first  responders as necessary, to ensure these agencies are familiar with the facility and its security plans, policies, and procedures.
Develop and implement procedures to review the security plan, especially any revisions that have been implemented within the past year, with local law enforcement on an annual basis.
Consult Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans, available at http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, for more information.

vulnerability_text: Key personnel are not aware of, or do not have access to, the security plan.

options.option_text: Ensure key personnel are aware of, and have a copy of, the security plan.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Develop a formal security awareness training program to educate all personnel on their security-related responsibilities. Include in the program topics such as the following: procedures for reporting suspicious activity, security incident (e.g. bomb threat, active shooter) response procedures, access control, and end-of-day security checks.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Document the completion of training for all employees.

Consult The Critical Success Factor Method: Establishing a Foundation for Enterprise Security Management, available at http://www.ready.gov/document/critical-success-factor-method-establishing-foundation-enterprise-security-management-0, for more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not train personnel on the security plan.

options.option_text: Develop and implement an initial and annual recurring security awareness training program for all management personnel, employees, and contractors. Consult the Ready.gov Website for more information on training, at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Train all personnel on the security plan annually. Consult the Ready.gov Website for more information on training, at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Train employees to do the following: identify their responsibilities under the facility's security program; recognize connections between the security program's objectives and selected security measures; remain familiar with resources for carrying out security-related responsibilities; and be prepared for security incidents. Consult the Ready.gov Website for more information on training, at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Develop a security awareness program that addresses the following topics:
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Why the facility requires protection strategies
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: What actions are required to protect specific assets and areas
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: What employees’ security responsibilities are and how they can be fulfilled
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: How employees can report program violations
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: How employees can identify indicators of risk or danger and how to respond appropriately
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Train employees on suspicious activity reporting procedures, security incident (e.g. bomb threat, active shooter) response procedures, procedures for suspicious packages, access control, and end-of-day security checks. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/emergency.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Enhance the level of employee security awareness through the distribution of a security-related newsletter, emails, and posters that address threats, concerns, training opportunities, security initiatives, and problem areas. Consult The Critical Success Factor Method: Establishing a Foundation for Enterprise Security Management, available at https://iacd.iac.anl.gov/document/,DanaInfo=www.ready.gov+critical-success-factor-method-establishing-foundation-enterprise-security-management-0, for more information.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Document the completion of training for all employees.

vulnerability_text: The facility trains some, but not all, personnel on the security plan.

options.option_text: Train all personnel on the security plan annually. Consult the Ready.gov Website for more information on training, at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Document the completion of training for all employees.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not exercise its security plan annually.

options.option_text: Test the security plan regularly with drills and tabletop exercises to ensure that adequate resources are available to implement the plan and that all operating units can perform their responsibilities as defined in the plan.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Exercise the plan at least once annually to ensure it remains applicable as changes occur at the facility. Update the plan as needed.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Exercise the plan regularly with employees and other appropriate personnel (e.g., building management, tenants) to ensure that adequate resources are available to implement the plan and that all operating units can perform their responsibilities as defined in the plan.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Involve first responders in exercises to familiarize them with the facility and its security plans, policies, and procedures.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Consult the Ready.gov Website for more information, at http://www.ready.gov/business/testing/exercises.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not document the results of security plan exercises.

options.option_text: Document the results of security plan exercises. Consult the Ready.gov Website for more information on training, at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training.
Implement procedures for capturing and implementing lessons learned. Consult the Ready.gov Website for more information on program improvement, at http://www.ready.gov/business/program.
Consult the Ready.gov Website for more information, at http://www.ready.gov/business/testing/exercises.

options.option_text: Active shooter

Develop a response plan and training program to provide guidance on how to respond to an active shooter. Refer to the DHS website for resources, including online training, booklets, and posters, at http://www.dhs.gov/active-shooter-preparedness.
Develop an active shooter protocol in close coordination with local first responders.
Provide initial and annual recurring active shooter training for all personnel.
Review and exercise the active shooter plan annually with all personnel.
Designate “safe rooms” throughout the facility. Ensure each room can be locked from within and has a telephone for communications with first responders.
Provide local first responders with a copy of the facility's floor plans to enhance their response capability in an active shooter incident.
Provide local first responders with a tour of the facility to familiarize them with its layout.
Internal disturbances (e.g., workplace violence)

Develop a protocol to respond to workplace violence. Refer to the FBI Website for resources, such as the January 2011 FBI Law Enforcement Bulletin: Workplace Violence Prevention, available at http://www.fbi.gov/stats-services/publications/law-enforcement-bulletin/january2011/january_2011.
Provide training on workplace violence to all personnel at initial hire and annually thereafter.

Refer to the FEMA Emergency Management Institute's Independent Study Program training course IS-906: Workplace Security Awareness. This free, 1-hour course provides guidance to individuals and organizations on how to improve security in the workplace and is available at http://training.fema.gov/EMIWeb/IS/IS906.asp.
Security communications policy or procedures
Information protection/OPSEC
Personnel security
Criminal activities (e.g., break-ins)
Hostage situations
A security awareness training program that addresses terrorist incidents

vulnerability_text: The facility lacks procedures for handling suspicious packages.

options.option_text: Develop procedures for handling suspicious packages.
Incorporate procedures on how to handle suspicious packages into initial and annual security training programs.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Implement U.S. Postal Service mail center security procedures to assess, prevent, and respond to threats such as suspicious letters and packages; bomb threats; and chemical, biological, or radiological contamination.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Prominently display informational materials on suspicious package indicators in the mailroom.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Develop a protocol to prevent air contamination (e.g., turn off fans or ventilation units, shut down the air handling system in the facility). Refer to the Ready.gov Website for more information about biological and chemical threats, at http://www.ready.gov/biological-threats, and http://www.ready.gov/chemical-threats.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Refer to the Ready.gov Website for information about suspicious packages and letters, at http://www.ready.gov/explosions.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not communicate security information to personnel.

options.option_text: Develop a communication plan that covers voice and data transfer of security-related information to all personnel.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Develop a process for communicating the current security situation and reminders of appropriate response procedures to all personnel. Include procedures for communicating with personnel who are not on site during an incident.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Enhance security awareness through the distribution of security-related newsletter and emails, and the display of security-related information (e.g., posters) that address potential threats, training opportunities, initiatives, and concerns.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Regularly test notification protocols through drills and exercises.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

vulnerability_text: There is no capability or process for an employee to report a security concern.

options.option_text: Establish a system for reporting security concerns. Include mechanisms for the documentation, investigation, and review of related incidents and actions taken.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Provide one or more options (e.g., hotline, direct phone or radio communications with security operations) for personnel to report security concerns (e.g., suspicious activity, potential threat, incident).

vulnerability_text: The facility does not participate in any security working groups.

options.option_text: Join relevant security working groups. Establish liaisons and regular communications with Federal, State, local, private sector, and/or industry working groups to enhance information sharing, track threat conditions, and support investigations.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not conduct any background checks.

options.option_text: Conduct background checks on all employees.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Investigation, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Conduct initial and recurring background checks on all employees.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Investigation, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Conduct initial and recurring background checks on all employees working in positions of trust or who are assigned to critical or sensitive areas.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Investigation, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Require contracting companies, temporary employment agencies, and/or vendors to conduct background checks on their personnel who will work at and/or visit the facility and to make such records available for audit.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Investigation, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

vulnerability_text: Although the facility conducts background checks, the type of checks conducted provides minimal information, creating a false sense of security in the workplace.

options.option_text: Explore options to conduct more comprehensive background checks to gather detailed information about personnel, especially those working in positions of trust or who are assigned to critical or sensitive areas.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Investigation, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Conduct national criminal history background checks to thoroughly investigate personnel. Review credit reports especially for personnel in financial management positions where there is potential for fraud and embezzlement.
options.reference: ASIS International, 2014, Auditing Management Systems: Risk, Resilience, Security, and Continuity—Guidance for Application (ANSI/ASIS SPC.2-2014), Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Seek appropriate consultation, for example, with your legal department, for guidance on conducting comprehensive background checks.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not conduct background checks on all employees.

options.option_text: Conduct background checks on all employees.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Investigation, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Conduct background checks on all employees, including temporary employees.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Investigation, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Conduct background checks on all employees, not only employees identified as critical to facility operations.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Investigation, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

vulnerability_text: Although the facility conducts background checks on employees, checks are conducted only at initial hire. Recurring background checks are not conducted.

options.option_text: Conduct recurring background check on employees.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Investigation, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Conduct recurring background checks on employees as appropriate. For example, recurring background checks may be necessary for employees working in positions of trust or assigned to critical or sensitive areas. Recurring background checks may occur on an annual basis, randomly, or on special occasions (e.g., when an employee is eligible for promotion).
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Investigation, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not conduct background checks on employee security personnel.

options.option_text: Conduct background checks on security personnel.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Investigation, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

vulnerability_text: Although the facility conducts background checks on employee security personnel, checks are conducted only at initial hire. Recurring background checks are not conducted.

options.option_text: Conduct recurring background checks on security personnel.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Investigation, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

vulnerability_text: Background checks are not conducted on contract security personnel.

options.option_text: Conduct background checks on contract security personnel.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Investigation, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Require security contracting companies to conduct background checks on their personnel who will work at the facility and to make such records available for audit.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Investigation, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

vulnerability_text: Although background checks are conducted on contact security personnel, checks are conducted only at initial hire. Recurring background checks are not conducted.

options.option_text: Ensure recurring background checks are conducted on contract security personnel.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Investigation, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

vulnerability_text: Background checks are not conducted on contractors.

options.option_text: Conduct background checks on all contractors.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Investigation, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Conduct background checks on contractors, especially those working in positions of trust or assigned to critical or sensitive areas.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Investigation, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Require contracting companies to conduct background checks on their personnel who will work at the facility and to make such records available for audit.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Investigation, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

vulnerability_text: Although background checks are conducted on contactors personnel, checks are conducted only at initial hire. Recurring background checks are not conducted.

options.option_text: Ensure recurring background checks are conducted on contractors.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Investigation, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Ensure recurring background checks are conducted on contractors as appropriate. For example, recurring background checks may be necessary for contractors working in positions of trust or assigned to critical or sensitive areas. Recurring background checks may occur on an annual basis, randomly, or on special occasions (e.g., when an contractor is eligible for reassignment).
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Investigation, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

vulnerability_text: Background checks are not conducted on vendors.

options.option_text: Require vendors to conduct background checks on their personnel who will work at the facility. Obtain proof from vendors that appropriate background checks have been conducted.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Investigation, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

vulnerability_text: Although background checks are conducted on vendors, checks are conducted only at initial hire. Recurring background checks are not conducted.

options.option_text: Require vendors to conduct recurring background checks on their personnel who will work at the facility. Obtain proof from vendors that recurring background checks are conducted.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Investigation, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

vulnerability_text: The facility uses and maintains sensitive information but does not identify or protect it.

options.option_text: Identify, mark, and protect sensitive information. Consult Building Security in the Digital Economy, available at  http://www.ready.gov/sites/default/files/documents/files/building_security_in_digital_economy.pdf, and Protecting Aggregated Data, available at  http://www.ready.gov/document/protecting%C2%A0aggregated%C2%A0data, for more information.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Information Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

vulnerability_text: The facility utilizes, maintains, and identifies sensitive information but does not protect it in any manner. As a result, this information may be easily obtainable in person or online.

options.option_text: Secure all sensitive information as appropriate (e.g., locked file cabinets, locked room).
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Information Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Restrict access to sensitive information, for example, with password protection.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Information Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Create policies and procedures to adequately destroy sensitive information (e.g., shredding, burning).
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Information Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

options.option_text: Apply markings to sensitive information to identify its protection requirements.
Create policies and procedures for secure transmission of sensitive information.
Require review of information disseminated to the public (e.g., internet postings) to ensure sensitive information is not inadvertently released.

Consult Building Security in the Digital Economy, available at  http://www.ready.gov/sites/default/files/documents/files/building_security_in_digital_economy.pdf, and Protecting Aggregated Data, available at  http://www.ready.gov/document/protecting%C2%A0aggregated%C2%A0data, for more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not store sensitive information securely. As a result, this information may be easily accessible.

options.option_text: Secure all sensitive information as appropriate (e.g., locked file cabinets, locked room). Consult Protecting Aggregated Data, available at http://www.ready.gov/document/protecting%C2%A0aggregated%C2%A0data, for more information.
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Information Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not limit electronic access to sensitive information through the use of passwords. As a result, this information may be easily accessible.

options.option_text: Restrict access to sensitive information, for example, with password protection. Consult Building Security in the Digital Economy, available at http://www.ready.gov/sites/default/files/documents/files/building_security_in_digital_economy.pdf, for more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not adequately destroy sensitive information to ensure that it cannot be accessed after disposal.

options.option_text: Create policies and procedures to adequately destroy sensitive information (e.g., shredding, burning).
options.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Information Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not mark sensitive information to ensure associated handling and safeguarding requirements are identified.

options.option_text: Apply markings to sensitive information to identify its protection requirements.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not ensure the secure transmission of sensitive information.

options.option_text: Create policies and procedures for secure transmission of sensitive information. Consult Building Security in the Digital Economy, available at http://www.ready.gov/sites/default/files/documents/files/building_security_in_digital_economy.pdf, for more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not require a security review of information disseminated to the public (e.g., Internet postings).

options.option_text: Require review of information disseminated to the public (e.g., internet postings) to ensure sensitive information is not inadvertently released. Consult Protecting Aggregated Data, available at  http://www.ready.gov/document/protecting%C2%A0aggregated%C2%A0data, for more information.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have security containers for the storage of sensitive information.

options.option_text: Procure and utilize containers that meet the security requirements of sensitive information stored at the facility.
Locate security containers where they can be observed by security personnel during their rounds.
If security containers with combination locks are necessary, implement the following protocols when they are obtained:
Record and store combinations.
options.reference: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc.

options.option_text: Change combinations regularly.
options.reference: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc.

options.option_text: Change combinations when personnel are terminated or moved.
options.reference: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not regularly change the combinations for its security containers.

options.option_text: Develop a process to change all security-container combinations on a schedule (e.g., semi-annually, annually).
options.reference: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not change the combinations for its security containers when significant personnel changes occur.

options.option_text: Develop a process to change all security-container combinations when personnel are terminated, depart from the organization, and so forth.
options.reference: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not record or secure combinations for its security containers.

options.option_text: Record and secure combinations for security containers.
options.reference: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc.

vulnerability_text: The facility does not have a perimeter fence. The lack of perimeter fencing may allow unrestricted access to the facility and critical areas within the facility.

options.reference: Fischer, R.J., E.P. Halibozek, and D.C. Walters, 2013, Introduction to Security, 9th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc, p. 229-239; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 91-113.

Fischer, R.J., E.P. Halibozek, and D.C. Walters, 2013, Introduction to Security, 9th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc, p. 229-239; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 39.
DOD, 2013, Unified Facilities Criteria (UFC) - Security Fences and Gates (UFC 4-022-03), section 2-12, p. 26-28, October, http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf, accessed July 10, 2014.

DOD, 2013, Unified Facilities Criteria (UFC) - Security Fences and Gates (UFC 4-022-03), section 2-11, p. 24-25, October, http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf, accessed July 10, 2014; FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-54, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-61, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014; FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), Appendix A, p. A-1 to A-6, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

vulnerability_text: Less than 100% of the facility is enclosed. The lack of fencing may allow unrestricted access to the facility.

options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 2.4.6, p. 2-73 to 2-74, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 60.

vulnerability_text: Although 100% of the facility is enclosed, 100% of SAAs are not enclosed. The lack of fencing may allow unrestricted access to critical areas of the facility.
Less than 100% of the facility is enclosed, and less than 100% of SAAs are enclosed. The lack of fencing may allow unrestricted access to critical areas of the facility.
A standard aluminum or steel chain-link fence may be easily traversed and provides little penetration delay against determined intruders.

options.reference: Fischer, R.J., E.P. Halibozek, and D.C. Walters, 2013, Introduction to Security, 9th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 229-239; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 91-113.

vulnerability_text: A wood fence may be only for demarcating a boundary or for decoration, but not for security purposes. A wood fence may be easily traversed or penetrated by determined intruders.
A plastic fence may be only for demarcating a boundary or for decoration, but not for security purposes. A plastic fence may be easily traversed or penetrated by determined intruders.
Fences less than 7 feet tall do little more than demarcate a boundary.

A fence between 5-6 feet in height is not suitable for security purposes. A security fence should be no less than 7 feet (excluding enhancements). A shorter fence can be easily traversed by intruders.
The base of the fence at the facility is not anchored, which may allow access under the fence line.

options.reference: DOD, 2013, Unified Facilities Criteria (UFC) - Security Fences and Gates (UFC 4-022-03), section 2-2, p. 15, October, http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf, accessed July 10, 2014.
DOD, 2013, Unified Facilities Criteria (UFC) - Security Fences and Gates (UFC 4-022-03), section 2-2, p. 15, October, http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf, accessed July 10, 2014.

vulnerability_text: Although the fence is equipped with outriggers, they are angled toward the facility and do not effectively deter access over the top of the fence from outside the perimeter.
The fence is not topped with outriggers, which makes it more scalable.

options.reference: DOD, 2013, Unified Facilities Criteria (UFC) - Security Fences and Gates (UFC 4-022-03), section 2-9.2, p. 22-24, October, http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf, accessed July 10, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The fence is topped with outriggers (support arms), but connecting wire (e.g., barbed or razor wire) is not installed on the supporting arms. Without connecting wire, the outriggers serve only as extensions of the fence posts.
The fence lacks privacy screening to obscure the observation of onsite facilities, critical  equipment, etc.

Although the fence provides a clear boundary marker, identifies restricted areas, and penetration delay, additional enhancements can provide additional penetration delay of the fence line (e.g., traversal, vehicle ramming) and more time for assessment.

options.reference: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 271, p. 275; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 263.
Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 339-353.
Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 270-271.
DOD, 2013, Unified Facilities Criteria (UFC) - Security Fences and Gates (UFC 4-022-03), section 2-4, p. 19, October, http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf, accessed July 10, 2014.
DOD, 2013, Unified Facilities Criteria (UFC) - Security Fences and Gates (UFC 4-022-03), section 2-4, p. 19, October, http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf, accessed July 10, 2014.

FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 2.4.1, p. 2-29, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.
DOD, 2013, Unified Facilities Criteria (UFC) - Security Fences and Gates (UFC 4-022-03), section 2-12.8, p. 34-37, October, http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf, accessed July 10, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The fence lacks a clear zone. A perimeter without a clear zone may aid intruders in gaining undetected access to a facility by providing areas for cover and concealment.

options.reference: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 272; Fischer, R.J., E.P. Halibozek, and D.C. Walters, 2013, Introduction to Security, 9th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 229-239; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 39, p. 91-113, p. 259.
Muuss, J.P., and D. Rabern, 2006, The Complete Guide for CPP Examination Preparation, Boca Raton, FL: Auerbach Publications, p. 174.
Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 3-7, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

vulnerability_text: There are objects and/or structures adjacent to the fence that may aid traversing the fence.

options.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 9-5, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

vulnerability_text: The fence is not clearly marked or identified with warning signs.

options.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 2.4.4, p. 2-70, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

vulnerability_text: There are no gates at openings in the fence line. The lack of gates provides individuals and vehicles with unrestricted access to the facility.

options.reference: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 112-113, p. 270; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 264-265.
Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 339-353.

vulnerability_text: The facility has a manually operated drop-arm gate that provides little to no protection against vehicle penetration.
Moveable bollards may be an option to slow traffic, but they would be unable to completely stop a vehicle traveling at a high rate of speed unless they were hydraulically operated.
The vehicle gate is not topped with outriggers, which makes the gate more scalable.

Although the vehicle gate provides a clear boundary marker and alerts individuals to restricted areas, the gate lacks enhancements that would increase its effectiveness to deter and delay penetration (e.g., traversal, vehicle ramming).
The vehicle gate is less than 7 feet tall. Gates less than 7 feet tall may be easily traversed.
The vehicle gate is not clearly marked or identified with warning signs.
There are no gates at pedestrian openings in the fence line. The lack of gates provides individuals with unrestricted access to the facility.
The pedestrian gate is not topped with outriggers, which makes the gate more scalable.

Although the pedestrian gate provides a clear boundary marker and alerts individuals to restricted areas, the gate lacks enhancements that would increase its effectiveness to deter unauthorized access to the facility.
The pedestrian gate is less than 7 feet tall. Gates less than 7 feet tall may be easily traversed.
The pedestrian gate is not clearly marked or identified with warning signs.
There are no gates at rail access points to the rail yard and yard facilities. The lack of such gates may allow unrestricted access to the rail yard and yard facilities.
The rail gate lacks enhancements that would increase its effectiveness to deter unauthorized rail access to the facility. Additional enhancements can prevent defeat of the gate.
